entries.question: 1. What is the correct billing for fluoro-guided lumbar puncture? 2. What is the ruling on patients being referred by a GP for an interventional procedure. (Question linked to code 50130-X-ray guided lumbar puncture)
entries.answer: 1. 50130 X-ray guided lumbar puncture plus consumables 2. We have no guidelines on referral of interventional procedures. Interventional cases can be referred to a radiologist for correct indications by anyone. HPCSA rules allow patients to see a specialist without a referral from anyone. The medical schemes are, however , able to make their own rules with respect to benefits, and could require a specialist referral.

entries.question: 1. What is the difference between the following codes? 32310 and 30360 2. What is the correct billing for a CT chest, inhaling and exhaling? 3. May codes 30320 & 32310 be combined?
entries.answer: 1. 32310 is only used for suspected congenital or post-traumatic abnormalities, 30360 for pulmonary embolism. 2. 30355 CT of the chest, complete high resolution study with additonal prone and expiratory studies 3. The general principle is that one code is used. CT pulmonary angiogram may not be combined with a CT chest, so following this precedent and the dictum of one examination it is reasonable to conclude that 32310 and 30320 should not be billed together and that 32310 is a stand-alone code and would include examination of the chest.

entries.question: 1.s calcium scoring part and parcel/included in a CTCA scan? 2. Is it appropriate for the radiologiy practice to charge for a non-contrasted CT chest as a proxy for the calcium scoring?
entries.answer: 33300 CT Anatomical/Function study of heart for Calcium scoring only 33310 CT Angiography of the heart vessels for full examination including calcium scoring 33300 and 33310 cannot be charged together.

entries.question: 3D Reconstruction Codes
entries.answer: Are 3D reconstruction codes billable, or are they considered redundant and included in the bast CT Code? We have a longstanding agreement with the medical schemes and have advised members that the following codes are redundant, as 3D reconstruction is part of every examination when required. The list of redundant codes is: Code Description 11310 CT of the facial bones with 3D reconstructions 14320 CT mandible with 3D reconstructions 15310 CT of the temporo-mandibular joints plus 3D reconstructions 55320 CT of the bony pelvis complete 3D recon 56320 CT of hip, complete with 3D recon 61310 CT of the left shoulder, complete with 3D recon 61315 CT of the right shoulder, complete with 3D recon 63310 CT of the left elbow, complete with 3D recon 63315 CT of the right elbow, complete with 3D recon 65310 CT of the left wrist and hand, complete with 3D recon 65315 CT of the right wrist and hand, complete with 3D recon 72310 CT of the left knee, complete study with 3D recon 72315 CT of the right knee, complete study with 3D recon 74310 CT of the left ankle/foot, complete with 3D recon 74315 CT of the right ankle/foot, complete with 3D recon When required, the code for the part examined should be used with the lesser units, for example, 11300 instead of 11310. This does not apply to the angio codes: Code Description Value 10540 Arteriography of intracranial vessels (four vessels) plus 3D reconstruction 10550 Arteriography of intracranial vessels (single vessel) plus 3D reconstruction

entries.question: Are speciallists that are not ENT's allowed to request a CT Limited Sinuses (Question linked to code 13300-CT of the paranasal sinuses single plane, limited study)
entries.answer: Any GP or specialist can request limited sinuses, that is the intention of the code to replace conventional sinus X-Rays.

entries.question: Are there any age limited on this code? (Question linked to code 10220-Ultrasound of the intracranial vasculature, including B mode, pulse and colour doppler)
entries.answer: There are no age rule that applies to this code.

entries.question: Are there cases where it is allowed for 70200 to be billed more than once? (Question linked to code 70200-Ultrasound lower limb – soft tissue - any region)
entries.answer: 70200 Ultrasound lower limb is only allowed once. There are separate codes for ultrasound right knee, left knee, right foot, left ankle etc.

entries.question: At what age should one start using code 10210 (US of the brain incl Doppler) instead of code 10200 (Neonatal US) (Question linked to code 10200-Ultrasound of the brain – Neonatal)
entries.answer: 10200 Ultrasound of the brain – Neonatal 10210 Ultrasound of the brain including doppler 10220 Ultrasound of the intracranial vasculature, including B mode, pulse and colour doppler These codes are out of date and in the current review have been bundled int a single code. Until this is published, we would recommend that 10200 is used for the routine neonatal brain and although doppler may be used routinely, it should not be added unless there is a specific clinical indication for it. 00210 Ultrasound with mobile unit in other facility - may be added if appropriate. We would also recommend the use of this code as long as the anterior fontanelle is open and the examination is possible, rather than attempting to stick to a definition of the neonatal period which is ofter difficult to quantify due to prematurity. 10200 Ultrasound of the brain – Neonatal Rule – may be billed for babies under 12 months old 10210 – Ultrasound of the brain including Doppler Rule – may be billed for babies under 12 months old 10220 – Ultrasound of the intracranial vasculature, including B mode, pulse and colour Doppler Rule – no age rule applies to this code I suspect the problem (rejection), comes in when: • The Radiology practice bills for either code 10200 or 10210 for a baby who has not yet been registered with the medical scheme. The account is submitted under the mother’s name, and the system rejects the claim due to the age of the mother. Once the baby is registered with the scheme, the claim can be reprocessed under the baby’s name, and the claim will pay correctly.

entries.question: Billing Guidelines for Codes 00155, 01040 & 00150
entries.answer: We have received several queries from medical schemes and administrators regarding the appropriate billing and use of the following codes: • 01040 – Radiographer attendance in theatre, per half hour • 00155 – X-ray C-arm fluoroscopy in theatre per half hour (procedure only) Following discussions with the schemes, the RSSA has reached an agreement allowing these codes to be billed together. However, the total time billed must not exceed the total theatre time. Additionally, schemes and their assessors can access theatre records and may use these to verify the total quantum billed by radiology practices. Therefore, it is essential that billing accurately reflects actual time spent in the theatre. Please note the following important guidelines: 1. Billing these codes together: You are entitled to bill both codes for each half hour (or part thereof) that the radiographer is in attendance in theatre. 2. Billing for time spent in theatre: o Code 00155 and code 01040 may be billed together for every half hour or part thereof that the radiographer is present in the theatre. o Radiographers are often required to stay in the theatre for intermittent screening; however, billing should reflect only the actual time spent in the theatre, not the entire procedure duration. 3. No additional regional screening codes: You may not add a code for the region being screened (e.g., 56120 – pelvis and hips) or additional control films (00135 – control film in theatre). 4. Restrictions on 01040 (Radiographer Attendance in Theatre): o This code may not be used for procedures performed in facilities owned by the radiologist. o It applies only to attendance in hospital theatres and does not apply to bedside unit (BSU) examinations. Guidelines for code 00150 - X-ray C-Arm (equipment fee) 1. Ownership requirement: Code 00150 should only be billed when the radiology practice owns the equipment. 2. Billing frequency: Code 00150 should be billed per half hour (or part thereof). However, the total time billed must not exceed the total theatre time. 3. Hospital-owned equipment: o If the hospital owns the C-arm, the hospital will bill the patient for its use. o The hospital will likely use a 4-digit SAMA code to recover C-arm equipment costs. o Hospitals cannot use the radiology 5-digit code (00150) to recover these costs, as radiology codes may only be billed by radiologists or nuclear physicians.

entries.question: Can 00155 and 01040 be charged together?
entries.answer: You are entitled to bill these 2 codes together for each half hour (or part thereof) that the radiographer is in attendance in theatre. Updated 1. Please note that you may not add on a code(s) for the region that you are screening e.g. 56120 (pelvis and hips) or code 00135 (control film in theatre). 2. Furthermore - some medical aids will query whether code 00155 (and code 01040) may be charged for the full time spent in theatre or just the actual screening time? a. Code 00155 (code 01040) may be charged for every half hour or part there of, spent in theatre. Radiographers often required to stay in theatre for intermittent short bursts of screening, but she/he must be there when surgeon wants screening, so he/she is tied to the theatre for a significant part of the total theatre time. On the other hand, you cannot bill for the whole theatre procedure time, as some, especially the radiographer owned practices do. Only time actually spent in theatre by the Radiographer may be billed.

entries.question: Can 10430 and 51420 be billed in combination for multiple sclerosis. (Question linked to code 10430-MR of the brain pre and post contrast)
entries.answer: See latest RSSA Multiple Sclerosis Guidelines.

entries.question: Can 10430 and 51420 be billed in combination for multiple sclerosis? (Question linked to code 10430-MR of the brain pre and post contrast)
entries.answer: See latest RSSA Multiple Sclerosis Guidelines.

entries.question: Can 30340 and 30360 be billed together. (Question linked to code 30340-CT of the chest, limited high resolution study)
entries.answer: 1. Code 30340 (CT limited high resolution) may be combined with 30310 or 30320 or 30330 (CT chest). Motivation may be required. 2. Code 30360, (CT chest for pulmonary embolism) is a complete examination and includes the preceding uncontrasted CT scan of the chest, and may not be combined with 40330 or 40333 (CT abdomen and pelvis). Regarding: Code 30360, (CT chest for pulmonary embolism) is a complete examination and includes the preceding uncontrasted CT scan of the chest, and may not be combined with 40330 or 40333 (CT abdomen and pelvis).

entries.question: Can 30340 and 30360 be billed together. (Question linked to code 30360-CT of the chest for pulmonary embolism)
entries.answer: 1. Code 30340 (CT limited high resolution) may be combined with 30310 or 30320 or 30330 (CT chest). Motivation may be required. 2. Code 30360, (CT chest for pulmonary embolism) is a complete examination and includes the preceding uncontrasted CT scan of the chest, and may not be combined with 40330 or 40333 (CT abdomen and pelvis). Regarding: Code 30360, (CT chest for pulmonary embolism) is a complete examination and includes the preceding uncontrasted CT scan of the chest, and may not be combined with 40330 or 40333 (CT abdomen and pelvis).

entries.question: Can 34110/34120 be charged in combination with 34130. (Question linked to code 34110-X-ray mammography study for localisation)
entries.answer: Rule Codes 34110 (localization), 34120 (stereo-tactic localization) and 34130 (stereo-tactic biopsy) may not be combined. Code 34130 (stereo-tactic biopsy). Add procedural code 80610 (cutting needle) or 34150 (mammotome) Code 34205 (U/S FNA) includes the procedural code (may not be combined with 34150). 34110 X-ray mammography study for localisation 34120 X-ray stereotactic mammography – localisation 34130 X-ray stereotactic mammography – biopsy You can only bill one of the appropriate codes.

entries.question: Can 34205 and 00230 be billed in combination? (Question linked to code 34205-Ultrasound guided aspiration FNA/localisation of the breast)
entries.answer: No. Both are ultrasound guidance codes.

entries.question: Can 70100 be charged twice if stress views were done of two separate regions? (Question linked to code 70100-X-ray lower limbs - any region- stress studies only)
entries.answer: Yes. Two separate studies can be billed. Regions examined should be clearly indicated on report and account.

entries.question: Can 70230 and 70240 be charged together. (Question linked to code 70230-Ultrasound peripheral venous system lower limbs including pulse and colour doppler for deep vein thrombosis)
entries.answer: Codes 70230 and 70240 may never be used together. 70240 Ultrasound peripheral venous system lower limbs including pulse and colour doppler in erect and supine position including all compression and reflux manoeuvres, deep and superficial systems bilaterally 70240 is used for the assessment of incompetence of the venous valves prior to treatment of varicose veins, exclusion of DVT is part of this examination, deep system is mentioned in the descriptor. Conversely 70240 may not be used simply for the exclusion of Deep Vein thrombosis.

entries.question: Can 70240 be billed instead of 70230 to compensate for right and left leg? (Question linked to code 70230-Ultrasound peripheral venous system lower limbs including pulse and colour doppler for deep vein thrombosis)
entries.answer: 70230 is the code for one or both legs for assessment of DVT. 70240 is for examination of the deep and superficial systems to assess varicose veins and the patency of the valves. It should not be used as a routine for the assessment of DVT.

entries.question: Can 80605 and 34205 be billed in combination? (Question linked to code 34205-Ultrasound guided aspiration FNA/localisation of the breast)
entries.answer: No. This is one of the anomalies in the current system, which has been corrected in the new. Suggest the following combination – 80605 + 00230 + Consumables At present FNA of the breast is a stand alone code and should be billed as: 34205 Ultrasound guided aspiration FNA/localisation of the breast Core biopsy of the breast uses the combination of: 00230 Ultrasound guidance 80610 Cutting needle, trochar biopsy, any region FNA any region except breast uses: 00230 Ultrasound guidance 80605 Fine needle aspiration biopsy, any region

entries.question: Can 85600 be charged twice if done during the same sitting. (Question linked to code 85600-Percutaneous nephrostomy, external drainage)
entries.answer: Machine fee once, 85600 may be charged right and left if both done. Should not be used twice for same side.

entries.question: Can 87669 be billed in comination with 00550? (Question linked to code 87669-Embolisation, uterus)
entries.answer: Any one may be added to any interventional code including 87669, but may be billed on its own when radiologist billing uses a hospital cath lab or another radiologists equipment. 87669 should be regarded as the professional fee.

entries.question: Can a 2 view chest X-ray be charged together with a Barium swallow for a patient with known Ca prostate? (Question linked to code 31110-X-ray barium swallow, double contrast)
entries.answer: A CXR is included in a Barium swallow study, so a single view chest x-ray may be billed together with 31110.

entries.question: Can a Barium swallow be billed together with a double contrast Barium meal? (Question linked to code 31100-X-ray barium swallow)
entries.answer: Although not specifically mentioned barium swallow is always included in a double contrast barium meal, the tariff code units cater for this.

entries.question: Can a diagnostic ultrasound be billed on the same day as code 00230
entries.answer: Yes, a diagnostic ultrasound can be billed on the same day as code 00230. The diagnostic ultrasound is performed to assess the need for a biopsy. Code 00230 includes the ultrasound performed for guidance during the biopsy procedure, not for diagnosis. The purpose of 00230's rule (Comprehensive ultrasound code including regional study and guidance) is to prevent duplicate billing for an additional diagnostic ultrasound done when the original diagnostic ultrasound was performed separately. It does not replace the initial diagnostic ultrasound, whether done on the same day or on a different day.

entries.question: Can a hand (65100/65105) be billed in combination with a wrist (65130/65135). (Question linked to code 65135-X-ray of the right wrist)
entries.answer: The rules do not preclude hand and wrist being billed together, when clinically indicated. This should not be done as a routine.

entries.question: Can a hand and finger be charged together? (Question linked to code 65120-X-ray of a finger)
entries.answer: The rule is that a finger or toe should not be charged if the hand or foot of the same side is done. A right hand and a left finger can be done and vv etc The rules state – “Code 65120 (finger) may not be combined with 65100 or 65105 (hands).” 65100 X-ray of the left hand 65105 X-ray of the right hand 65120 X-ray of a finger

entries.question: Can a lateral view of the spine at the time of the BMD study be billed for seperately? (Question linked to code 50120-X-ray bone densitometry)
entries.answer: Some models of Bone densitometry screening equipment are able to produce a lateral view of the spine at the time of the BMD study. This is considered to be part of the study and separate billing for this image as a spine view is not acceptable.

entries.question: Can a PA and lateral chest be charged together with a Barium swallow? (Question linked to code 30110-X-ray of the chest two views, PA and lateral)
entries.answer: With suspected oesophageal motility disorders, aspiration is an associated problem. PA and lateral Xray indicated for this reason and with any symptoms related to the thoracic oesophagus.

entries.question: Can code 30360 be combined with any other code e.g. 40337 given than code 30360 is defined as a stand alone code? (Question linked to code 30360-CT of the chest for pulmonary embolism)
entries.answer: As a general rule the answer is "No" but there may be certain circumstances where it is appropriate (with motivation)

entries.question: Can code 30370 be combined with any other code e.g. 40337 given than code 30370 is defined as a stand alone code? (Question linked to code 30370-CT of the chest for pulmonary embolism with CT venography of abdomen, pelvis and lower limbs)
entries.answer: As a general rule the answer is "No" but there may be certain circumstances where it is appropriate (with motivation). Code 30370 (CT pulmonary embolism plus CT venography) may not be combined with 70230 (Doppler).

entries.question: Can codes 00145 and 60580 be billed together?
entries.answer: 00145 is the one of the imaging methods for biopsy so it may be added to 80600, 80605 and 80610. It should not be added to the angiography and venography codes.

entries.question: Can different radiology providers to claim separately in a case where one is the owner of the equipment and the other performed the procedure or should the owner of the equipment perform the procedure?
entries.answer: There would also be no problem if the owner of the equipment (radiologist) billed for the whole procedure and passed on the interventional component to the radiologist doing it. Coversely one radiologist could bill for the machine fee and the second radiologist for the procedure fee.

entries.question: Can MRI brain and MRI IAM codes be used in combination. (Question linked to code 16400-MR of the internal auditory canals, limited study)
entries.answer: MRI brain and MRI IAM codes should not be added. The brain series will always include the IAMS.

entries.question: Can MRI brain and MRI IAM codes be used in combination. (Question linked to code 16410-MR of the internal auditory canals, pre and post contrast, limited study)
entries.answer: MRI brain and MRI IAM codes should not be added. The brain series will always include the IAMS.

entries.question: Can MRI brain and MRI IAM codes be used in combination. (Question linked to code 16420-MR of the internal auditory canals, pre and post contrast, complete study)
entries.answer: MRI brain and MRI IAM codes should not be added. The brain series will always include the IAMS.

entries.question: Can one bill 00540 with a fluoroscopy procedure as equipment fee? 2. Does the fluoroscopy procedure then include the radiographer and radiologist time, the associated imaging and radiology report?
entries.answer: No, the Rule is: Codes 80600, 80605, 80610, 80620, 80630, 81660, 81680, 82600, 84660, 85640, 85645, 86610, 86615, 86620, 86630, (aspiration / biopsy / ablations etc) may be combined with the relevant guidance codes (fluoroscopy, ultrasound, CT, MR) as previously described. The machine codes 00510, 00520, 00530, 00540, 00550, 00560 may not be combined with these codes. If ultrasound guidance (00230) is used for a procedure which also attracts one of the machine codes (00510, 00520, 00530, 00540, 00550, 00560), it may not be billed for separately. Codes 80640, 80645, 87682, 87683 include fluoroscopy. Machine fees may not be added.

entries.question: Can x-ray of sacrum/coccyx be billed in combination with an X-ray of the pelvis? (Question linked to code 54100-X-ray of the sacrum and coccyx)
entries.answer: The codes involved are: 54100 X-ray of the sacrum and coccyx 55100 X-ray of the pelvis The technical answer is yes but it would be in very unusual circumstances and never routinely. Saccral Xray is notoriously poor for detecting sacral fractures. If suspected clinically or on plain film of pelvis, CT or MTR is the examination of choice, There are virtually no residual indications for x-ray of the coccyx.

entries.question: Clinical History: Left Pleural Effusion Please advise on codes for Ultrasound-Guided Pleural Pigtail Catheter Placement.
entries.answer: Billing is: 00230 Ultrasound guidance 82600 Chest drain insertion Plus disposables, plus CXR as required.

entries.question: Could you please clarify the reasoning behind code 00160 (X-Ray fixed theatre installation - equipment fee only) and code 00135 (X- Ray control view in theatre any region)?
entries.answer: 00160-typically in Urology theatre used for retrograde studies. Radiologist must own and maintain the equipment. If such a retrograde study is reported on by a Radiologist code 42155 must be billed for this report. 00135 is meant for views done during a procedure, over and above screening, e.g. view of a prosthesis post insertion or a lateral cervical spine after anterior fusion etc. May only be used if an actual stored image (film or PACS) if done during an procedure/operation and a report issued by the radiologist.

entries.question: CT venography lower limbs (Question linked to CT of the chest for pulmonary embolism with CT venography of abdomen, pelvis and lower limbs)
entries.answer: If combined with a CT pulmonary angiogram: 30370 CT of the chest for pulmonary embolism with CT venography of abdomen, pelvis and lower limbs If stand alone: 70310 CT angiography of the lower limb Angiography includes arterography and/or venography. Single charge not per side.

entries.question: Currently For pulmonary embolus, a CT angio protocol is utilised with the code: 30360 Can we use this code below when CT angio is requested for pulmonary embolus ? When else can we use this code below? 32310
entries.answer: When doing an examination for pulmonary embolism the appropriate code is 30360. 32310 should not be used for a pulmonary embolism study. The other code is largely redundant but is occasionally used for studies requiring anatomical definition of the pulmonary vasculature.

entries.question: Do codes 30360 and 30370 include Pre and Post Contrast studies? (Question linked to code 30360-CT of the chest for pulmonary embolism)
entries.answer: The rule which includes pre-contrast examinations applies to both 30360 and 30370. 30370 is simply 30360 plus venography. Code 30360, (CT chest for pulmonary embolism) is a complete examination and includes the preceding uncontrasted CT scan of the chest, and may not be combined with 40330 or 40333 (CT abdomen and pelvis). Code 30370 (CT pulmonary embolism plus CT venography) may not be combined with 70230 (Doppler).

entries.question: Do codes 56400, 56410 and 56420 (MRI hip joint/s) include both hip joints. (Question linked to code 56400-MR of the hip joint/s, limited study)
entries.answer: As per description, it includes both hips. However if dedicated studies with dedicated coils are done of the individual hips, especially if both are done with intra-articular contrast, then 2 x separate fees are justified. Each hip needs the time of a full study. If both hips are done simultaneously with the body coil only one x billing allowed.

entries.question: Does the 87669 uterine embolisation code include the angiogram code or can we charge 87669 with 44520?
entries.answer: 44507, 44520 and 44525 may not be added to 87669. All interventional procedures using angiography include the roadmap angiogram at the time of the procedure.

entries.question: Due to medical aids not paying for lumbar spine and coccyx billed together (53120 or 53110 + 54100), is there an alternative code that can be used. (Question linked to code 53120-X-ray of the lumbar spine, more than two views)
entries.answer: The common practice is to include sacrum and coccyx, when indicated in 53120. There are very few residual indications for plain film examination of the lumbar spine without trauma. There are virtually no residual indications for plain film examination of the sacrum and coccyx as coccygeal trauma is treated clinically irrespective of an X-ray abnormality. For these reasons we think that 53120 is sufficient and we do not support the bundling of sacrum and coccyx with lumbar spine,

entries.question: For codes 00510 – 00560 (Angio tables) – do these codes only comprise of a technical component, or does it include a professional component as well? (Question linked to code 00510-Analogue monoplane screening table)
entries.answer: These codes include all components (equipment, staffing, etc) except for the radiologist component (which is included in the 8xxxx procedure fees).

entries.question: Given the clinical history of abdominal pain only, and no indication from the referring practitioner that they are querying obstruction or perforation, 40100 appears to be the appropriate code. When should 40100 be used and when 40105.
entries.answer: 40100 is standard and 40105 used when perforation or obstruction is suspected.

entries.question: How can the issue of IVP vs. CT (for renal stones) be resolved, keeping in mind that medical aids don't pay for a CT requested by a GP. (Question linked to code 42110-X-ray excretory urogram including tomography)
entries.answer: The gold standard for the detection and exclusion of renal stones is non contrast CT. There is no indication for doing an IVU for stones when CT is available. The approach should be; 1. Ensure through CME etc that your supporters are aware of current practice. 2. In the meantime suggest that any potential examination for confirmation or exclusion of stones be discussed with an urologist and the urologist's name, with his or her permission, be used for the referral. 3. We will write to all the medical aids to suggest that those schemes that do not allow direct GP referral and funding of non contrast CT should do so both from a best practice and cost point of view. We will feed back with responses.

entries.question: If a bilateral venous Doppler was requested, should 70230 or 70240 be charged.
entries.answer: 70230 is the code for one or both legs for assessment of DVT. 70240 is for examination of the deep and superficial systems to assess varicose veins and the patency of the valves. It should not be used as a routine for the assessment of DVT.

entries.question: If a referring doctor in the hospital makes use of radiology's portable ultrasound machine, would code 00210 be the correct code to charge, although this is not a stand alone code and needs to be accompanied by a procedure code?
entries.answer: We suggest you charge mobile in full and 1/3 for part examined. This is the logical converse of rule: "Where public sector hospital equipment is used for a procedure, the units will be reduced by 33,33%".

entries.question: If the referring doctor requests CT Angio of the Thoracic and Abdominal aorta and runoff to the legs what code are we supposed to use? (Question linked to code 32305-CT angiography of the thoracic and abdominal aorta and branches)
entries.answer: Our recommendation before has been: 32305 CT angiography of the thoracic and abdominal aorta and branches 70310 CT angiography of the lower limb 32305 100% 70310 50% This was accepted by Discovery. Answered 07/11/2024

entries.question: If ultrasound-guided hookwire localisation is done of both axillae, can both sides be billed? (Question linked to code 33981-PET/CT scan of the heart?)
entries.answer: The guiding principle of the five digit coding system is that where possible there should be a single code per procedure. In the case of needle localisation of the breast or axilla there is a single code for this procedure which is 34205 (Ultrasound guided aspiration FNA/localisation of the breast) ; 80605 should not be added to this, there is no aspiration in this case and even if there was that is included in 34205. In the case where this is a bilateral or multiple code 34205 may be used more than once. The correct coding for this procedure is 34205 x 2.

entries.question: If ultrasound-guided hookwire localisation is done of both axillae, can both sides be billed? (Question linked to code 80605-Fine needle aspiration biopsy, any region)
entries.answer: The guiding principle of the five digit coding system is that where possible there should be a single code per procedure. In the case of needle localisation of the breast or axilla there is a single code for this procedure which is 34205 (Ultrasound guided aspiration FNA/localisation of the breast) ; 80605 should not be added to this, there is no aspiration in this case and even if there was that is included in 34205. In the case where this is a bilateral or multiple code 34205 may be used more than once. The correct coding for this procedure is 34205 x 2.

entries.question: If we charge c-arm in theatre, may we still charge radiographer attendance as well. (Question linked to code 01040-Radiographer attendance in theatre, per half hour)
entries.answer: As background – equipment code 00150 - X-ray C-Arm 1. Code 00150 should only be billed by the practice when the practice is the owner of the equipment 2. Code 00150 should be billed per half hour (or part thereof) 3. Should the hospital be the owner of the equipment, the hospital will bill the patient for the use of the C-arm. a. The hospital will most probably use a 4 digit SAMA code to recover their (hospital) c-arm equipment costs. b. Please note that the Radiology 5 digit codes can only be billed by radiologists / nuclear physicians i.e. the hospital cannot use code 00150 to recover the equipment cost Re codes 00155 & 01040 You are entitle to bill these 2 codes together for each half hour (or part thereof) that the radiographer is in attendance in theatre. 1. Please note that you may not add on a code(s) for the region that you are screening e.g. 56120 (pelvis and hips) or code 00135 (control film in theatre). 2. Furthermore – some medical aids will query whether code 00155 (and code 01040) may be charged for the full time spent in theatre or just the actual screening time? a. Code 00155 (code 01040) may be charged for every half hour or part there off, spent in theatre. Radiographers often required to stay in theatre for intermitted short bursts of screening, but she/he must be there when surgeon wants screening, so he/she is tied to the theatre for a significant part of the total theatre time. On the other hand, you cannot bill for the whole theatre procedure time, as some, especially the radiographer owned practices do. Only time actually spent in theatre by the Radiographer may be billed.

entries.question: In a case where a CT scan is done for surgical navigation or surgical planning, is there another billing code to be used? (Question linked to code 00310-CT planning study for radiotherapy)
entries.answer: Suggest using 00310 CT planning study for radiotherapy 00450 MR planning study for radiotherapy or surgical procedure with appropriate change in wording of account on 00310/00450

entries.question: In which cases should 42300 vs. 40337 be used? What is to be done if a doctor's standard request is 40337 (pre- and post-contrast)? (Question linked to code 42300-CT of the renal tract for a stone)
entries.answer: All cases of suspected renal colic, with or without haematuria should have non contrast CT, most cases of painless haematuria should have a CT IVU. If all cases with heamaturia are done non contrast the patients are being under-investigated, conversely if all patients with haematuria are investigated pre-and post-contrast with CT IVU they are being over investigated. The referring doctor’s request that all cases should have pre- and post-contract studies is not necessarily appropriate as this does not fit in with international guidelines. It is difficult to make a ruling on an individual case such as this. It is our suggestion that you should ask Verirad whether that this is a sporadic incidents or whether your practice profile is out of line with other practices. We once again state that we cannot support pre and post contrast studies as routine in every case of suspected renal colic.

entries.question: Is 34150 only a technical fee (equipment) or does it include the procedure as well. (Question linked to code 34150-X-ray Mammotome hand held biopsy apparatus)
entries.answer: Code 34205 (U/S FNA) includes the procedural code (may not be combined with 34150).

entries.question: Is 56140 a stress study only or does it include the X-ray of the hip. (Question linked to code 56100-X-ray of the left hip)
entries.answer: 56140 is a stress study only and may be combined with 56100, 56110 or 56120

entries.question: Is 56140 a stress study only or does it include the X-ray of the hip. (Question linked to code 56110-X-ray of the right hip)

entries.question: Is 56140 a stress study only or does it include the X-ray of the hip. (Question linked to code 56120-X-ray pelvis and hips)

entries.question: Is angiography included in the interventional procedure code? (Question linked to code 10510-Arteriography of intracranial vessels: 3 - 4 vessels)
entries.answer: A a general rule the interventional procedure includes the diagnostic angiogram done at the same sitting which is necessary to road map the interventional procedure. If an angiogram is done purely to make the diagnosis and or to assess the feasibility of intervention and is done at a separate sitting that is allowable. Often this is replaced by a CT or MR angio. There should be some flexibility in this approach but the intention is that the diagnostic angiogram should not be repeated at the time of intervention if the indications for intervention are clear and a diagnosis has been made.

entries.question: Is code 00310 a standalone code, or is it in addition to the area scanned (Question linked to code 00310-CT planning study for radiotherapy)
entries.answer: It is usually a stand-alone code, as the diagnostic code would have been used at another session to make the diagnosis or monitor progression. There could be occasions when they may need to be done simultaneously, but they would be unusual and probably require motivation.

entries.question: Is code 13320 only to be used for ENT referral and 13300 for any other provider. (Question linked to code 13300-CT of the paranasal sinuses single plane, limited study)
entries.answer: The intention was that an ENT surgeon can use 13300 and 13320. Other practitioners other than a ENT surgeon should use 13300

entries.question: Is it allowed to charge codes 40333 (CT Abdomen and Pelvis Post contrast) and 30360 (CT Chest for pulmonary embolism) together? - (Question linked to code 40330-CT of the abdomen and pelvis) 40330
entries.answer: Code 30360, (CT chest for pulmonary embolism) is a complete examination and includes the preceding uncontrasted CT scan of the chest, and may not be combined with 40330 or 40333 (CT abdomen and pelvis). The intention here is that he combination should not be charged routinely. There are times when it is appropriate and we support this combination when appropriately motivated e.g Oncology patient who needs re-staging and has symptoms suggestive of pulmonary embolism.

entries.question: Is it allowed to charge codes 40333 (CT Abdomen and Pelvis Post contrast) and 30360 (CT Chest for pulmonary embolism) together? - (Question linked to code 40333-CT of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast)
entries.answer: Code 30360, (CT chest for pulmonary embolism) is a complete examination and includes the preceding uncontrasted CT scan of the chest, and may not be combined with 40330 or 40333 (CT abdomen and pelvis). The intention here is that the combination should not be charged routinely. There are times when it is appropriate and we support this combination when appropriately motivated e.g Oncology patient who needs re-staging and has symptoms suggestive of pulmonary embolism

entries.question: Is it appropriate to charge 00410 if a ltd dorsal spine (52400) and ltd lumbar spine (53400) were done. (Question linked to code 53400-MR of the lumbar spine, limited study)
entries.answer: 00410 is appropriate if the only sequences were either a single saggital sequence or two saggital sequences, one for each part. If in addition to the saggital sequence one of more axial sequences were done for each segment the 52400 and 5300 codes are appropriate .

entries.answer: 00410 is appropriate if the only sequences were either a single saggital sequence or two saggital sequences, one for each part. If in addition to the saggital sequence one or more axial sequences were done for each segment the 52400 and 53400 codes are appropriate .

entries.question: Is it correct to bill codes 00230 (ultrasound guidance) and 80605 (FNAB) together?
entries.answer: 00230 is the guidance code and 80605 is the procedure code. Both may and should be billed together. Note the exception for the bundled code 34205 - Ultrasound guided aspiration FNA/localisation of the breast is used for ultrasound guide FNA or marker localisation.

entries.question: Is it correct to bill codes 00230 (ultrasound guidance) and 80605 (FNAB) together? (Question linked to code 80605-Fine needle aspiration biopsy, any region)
entries.answer: 00230 is the guidance code and 80605 is the procedure code. Both may and should be billed together. Note the exception for the bundled code 34205 - Ultrasound guided aspiration FNA/localisation of the breast is used for ultrasound guide FNA or marker localisation.

entries.question: Is it correct to only charge one calcaneus combined with both feet if both feet and both calcanei was requested.
entries.answer: The rule reads: Codes 74120 and 74125 (foot) may only be combined with 74130 and 74135 (calcaneus) if specifically requested.

entries.question: Is it standard practice to stop at calcium scoring only if the calcium score is 0 and which other clinical findings can be identified when continuing with the full CTCA. (Question linked to code 33300-CT anatomical/functional study of the heart)
entries.answer: There is a difference in calcium scoring in non symptomatic and symptomatic patients. In non symptomatic patients calcium scoring is used for screening or follow up as a stand alone exam. In symptomatic patients, as in your programme, it is used to assess the feasibility of performing a contrast CTCA. If the score is too high the CTCA is unreliable. If, in a symptomatic patient the calcium score is zero a contrast exam should still be performed as there could still be significant pathology without calcification. Our recommendation is that 33300 should be funded if it is the only exam performed but 33310 should include calcium scoring if done. i.e 33300 and 33310 should not be billed together.

entries.question: Is the following billing correct: FNA: 00230 and 80605 Core biopsy: 00230 and 80610 Hook needle insertion for tumor marking: 34110 and 34205
entries.answer: The general principle is that an imaging modality code plus procedure code is used. The exception to this is the single code: 34205 Ultrasound guided aspiration FNA/localisation of the breast is used for ultrasound guide FNA or marker localisation. There are no codes for either hookwire or marker insertion using imaging method other than ultrasound 80610 is a reasonable surrogate.

entries.question: Is there a code for CT chest, inhaling and exhaling. (Question linked to code 30355-CT of the chest, complete high resolution study with additonal prone and expiratory studies)

entries.answer: The sonar code would be used to assess a fluid collection around the catheter. If contrast was injected: 00120-X-ray sinogram any region would be applicable

entries.question: Is there a separate billing code for mammograms for male patients? (Question linked to code 34100-X-ray mammography including ultrasound)
entries.answer: The mammo code 34100 (X-ray mammography including ultrasound ) applies to both females & males. Medical aid should not reject the code because the patient is a male.

entries.question: Is there a tariff code for occlusion of more than one aneurysm? (Question linked to code 81600-Intracranial aneurysm occlusion, direct)
entries.answer: Following correspondence with Discovery Health and our recent discussion, Discovery Health has agreed to the following: In the existing structure there is a single code: 81600 Intracranial aneurysm occlusion, direct This includes one or more aneurysms. When more than one aneurism are done in one sitting some SANIS members have been re-billing the code for each aneurism . This is not appropriate as patient set up, introduction etc are common irrespective of number of aneurisms done. It is equally unreasonable that you should not be reimbursed when more than on aneurism is done. When more than one aneurysm is done, 50% of the fee should be charged for each additional aneurysm.

entries.question: Is there an additional billing code to be charged for a mammogram done after hookwire localisation to check the position of the wire. (Question linked to code 34110-X-ray mammography study for localisation)
entries.answer: Two view mammography is mandatory after mammographic localisation to confirm and document the position of the hook wire. It is however part of the procedure fee for 34110. 34100 cannot be added to this. 34100 should never be billed after ultrasound localisation as ultrasound is used to localise the lesion and confirm the position of the needle post localisation.

entries.question: Is there an additional code to be charged for a report done on a screening procedure in theatre. 2. Is there a code to be charged for a report on pyelography done in theatre.
entries.answer: If a written report is made on a fluoroscopic procedure in theatre there is no additional charge for the report. If however during the procedure still images are captured and either printed or stored in a PACS the fee for that part may be levied in addition to the fluoroscopic fee. The fee for reporting is included in this procedure fee. 42155 should be used for reporting a pyelogram done in theatre.

entries.question: Is there an alternative to charging of tariff code 00230 and 80605 when multiple lesions are biopsied, or can one of each of these codes be charged per sitting without the medical aid rejecting. (Question linked to code 00230-Ultrasound guidance)
entries.answer: Common sense usually guides us in these discussions. I have had a similar discussion with both Discovery and Verirad in the past. The most common scenario is in breast biopsy, but it may be used in any part of the body. Each biopsied lesion attracts the biopsy code 80605 0r 80610. The guidance code should be used once for each set-up. i.e if a breast is draped and one or more lesions are biopsied in that breast, the guidance code is charged once for that breast. However, if there are lesions in the other breast and the machine is repositioned and the breast draped, the biopsy codes are again charged for each lesion biopsied, but code 00230 once again. i.e. 00230 may be charged once for each ultrasound biopsy field used

entries.question: Is there an imaging code for the Slit Collimated Whole Body Radiography in Trauma (e.g.Lodox) scan currently used in some trauma units.
entries.answer: The RSSA’s policy on Lodox is: "Slit Collimated Whole Body Radiography in Trauma is out dated, unnecessary technology, which produces whole body-shoot through images in one plane. It will not adequately triage intracranial, spinal, intra-abdominal or severe intra-thoracic trauma. Lodox is not used to any extent in any health system world wide. Trauma throughout the world is dealt with by a combination of conventional digital radiology and the 'gold standard' multislice CT. Multislice CT tomograms will provide the same single plane image as Lodox but in addition provide detailed cross sectional imaging. A modern multislice CT can be installed in trauma units for a similar cost to Slit Collimated Whole Body Radiography in Trauma. The RSSA does not support Slit Collimated Whole Body Radiography in Trauma or similar technology and will not assist in or develop a code for its use.” The code you have suggested is totally inappropriate as Slit Collimated Whole Body Radiography in Trauma is not a MR examination.

entries.question: May code 00230 (Ultrasound Guidance) be charged for if an FNA goes on to be a core biopsy. Are these 2 separate procedures, or if done as a follow on, may the U/S Guidance be charged for again
entries.answer: A guidance code may only be billed once per sitting but a procedure code per nodule or level. In the case of a follow-on, the 34205 should be replaced by 00230 + 80610.

entries.question: May code 00320 (CT Guidance) be charged “per level” if nerve blocks are done, or is it a “once off” (irrespective of how many levels were infiltrated). (Question linked to code 00320-CT guidance (separate procedure))
entries.answer: Guidance fee – 00320 - to be charged only once per sitting, but the procedure code is billed per level.

entries.question: Nephrostomy procedures (CPT 85600) performed with biplane fluoroscopy (CPT 00560) - Downcoding Query
entries.answer: Practice Query regarding coding and reimbursement for percutaneous nephrostomy procedures (CPT 85600) performed with biplane fluoroscopy (CPT 00560). They are concerned about Verirad's request to downcode these procedures to ultrasound or conventional single-plane fluoroscopy codes (00230 or 00140), or to apply a 30% reduction. They highlight the technical complexity of biplane guidance and the potential clinical benefits it offers. Answer: The practice and radiologist must have full discretion to decide whether the case is suitable for monoplane or biplane equipment if X-ray imaging is required. Verirad has no authority to suggest an imaging modality. Verirad is also now in full agreement that there is no indication for discounting CT examinations by 30% for any CT procedure.

entries.question: Our practice would assist with an ultrasound guided placement of a CVP line should the referring doctor not be able to do so. Can we bill code 80645? (Question linked to code 80645-Peripheral central venous line insertion)
entries.answer: The Rule states: Codes 80640, 80645…… does not attract a Machine fees 00510 etc. may not be added, and that 00230 may be added. However, this general rule where machine codes include ultrasound guidance is outdated and no longer applicable. RSSA will support the addition of 00230 to a machine fee when ultrasound is used for guidance

entries.question: Please advise if code 41130 is included in code 84605 (Percutaneous external/internal biliary drainage). 2. Please advise if above codes can be billed for more than once if more than one drainage is done.
entries.answer: The general principle is that a diagnostic procedure should not be billed again at the time of an interventional procedure, however it is unlikely that 41130 will be used as a stand alone procedure so: If 41130 has not been performed 41130 and 84605 may be added. If multiple stents or drains are inserted the second and subsequent drains should be charged at 50% of 84605.

entries.question: Please advise if my billing of tariff codes are correct? Biopsy same day as Mammo: 34100 80610 00230 Biopsy on a different day as the mammo: 80610 00230 (or do I add 34205?)
entries.answer: Correct, 34205 should not be added, it is either 80610 and 00230 for core or he combined fee 34205 for FNA, not both.

entries.question: Please advise if the billing of code 87664 x 2 (Embolization of abdominal and other vessels) is allowable “because both right hepatic and middle haptic veins were embolized.
entries.answer: Of note, the code is: 87664 - Embolisation, abdominal, other vessels. ‘Vessels' is in the plural, and the descriptor does not include the words ‘per vessel’ It would be inappropriate to double the charge for two embolisations off the same stem vessel. The double charging of 87664 is not supported.

entries.question: Please advise on tariff codes applicable for an MRI of the brachial plexus
entries.answer: The first thing is that the procedure requires pre- and -post-contrast studies. The examination involves the nerves coming out of the cervical spine and passing under the clavicle to the arm. There is no code, but the best surrogate would be: 20410 MR of the soft tissue of the neck, pre and post contrast

entries.question: Please advise on the principle where a radiologist billed for code 84610 (Permanent biliary stent insertion) x 3?
entries.answer: Our rulings on embolisation and the direction we are taking with the new coding structure set a precedent, so I suggest 100% followed by two 50%.

entries.question: Please advise on the procedure code for radiologist reading of a mammogram (study performed elsewhere.) (Question linked to code 01050-Written report on study done elsewhere, short)
entries.answer: Code 01050 could be used for non-Discovery Health patients. For Discovery patients, code 01065 could be used

entries.question: Please provide clarity regarding Tarriff Code 30355: We would like to confirm whether is is a requisite for the expiratory high resolution scan to be done prone.
entries.answer: The codes are: 30340 CT of the chest, limited high resolution study 30350 CT of the chest, complete high resolution study 30355 CT of the chest, complete high resolution study with additonal prone and expiratory studies Medihelp îs correct: 30340: Single inspiratory view 30350: Inspiration and expiration supine 30355: as in 30350 plus prone study

entries.question: PPE claims to Discovery claims rejected for the following reason "Specific Providers only to Bill Code".
entries.answer: Radiologists to bill PPE under code 00090 and include NAPPI codes as per NAPPI code file. Other specialists / GPs to bill PPE under code PPE01.

entries.question: Query on RF ablation billing: Procedure is time-consuming, under-remunerated, and blocks CT for 1–2 hrs. Requesting (1) fee increase, (2) billing per lesion treated, (3) allowance for 2nd doctor fee, and (4) use of code 00340 for monitoring.
entries.answer: I am not sure why the schemes are not paying appropriately. We are using the current RSSA 5-digit coding structure, which is the last version recognised in the 2006 NHRPL. In the event of a dispute, the Council for Medical Schemes will refer back to that document. The following codes appear in the 2006 NHRPL: • 00340 – CT guidance and monitoring for tissue ablation • 80630 – Tumour ablation, radiofrequency, per lesion • 01030 – Radiologist assistance in theatre, per half hour All of these codes are used in RF tumour ablations. • 00340 should only be billed once • 80630 should be billed per lesion • 01030 should be billed per half hour You have the full support of the RSSA in using the codes as outlined above. Additionally, 01070 may be used in accordance with the RSSA guidelines. If you receive further rejections, please provide this advice to the schemes. Should they continue to reject the claims, you should lodge a formal complaint with the Council for Medical Schemes.

entries.question: Question - Please can you advise what the most appropriate code for a colonic transit study would be. . (Question linked to code 40100-X-ray of the abdomen)
entries.answer: There is no screening involved, unlike small bowel follow through. The RSSA suggest billing 40100 (X-ray of the abdomen) up to three times in the first 24 hours. After that, it should be billed once in each subsequent 24-hour period as required.

entries.question: Regarding to after hour scanning of stroke patients, is it correct to do an initial CT followed by MRI if no bleed is seen or does one do an MRI straight away? (Question linked to code 10450-MR of the brain plus angiography)

entries.question: What are the billing codes for the following procedures: 1. Disposable needle biopsy 2. Semi-auto biopsy gun 3. Vacuum biopsy What is the appropriate billing for breast biopsy and localisation.
entries.answer: These are confusing and have been sorted out in the new version. In their list the first three are disposable items so what is used in charged. Ultrasound guided biopsies are billed: FNA 34205 US Guided FNA (1st lesion) 34205 US Guided FNA (2nd lesion) etc CORE 00230 US Guidance (once) 80610 Cutting needle biopsy (Ist lesion, 2nd lesion) etc plus needle, vacuum gun, semi automatic gun etc In the second list the principle is that a guidance method is used and disposables are added. The rules cover these: Codes 34110 (localization), 34120 (stereo-tactic localization) and 34130 (stereo-tactic biopsy) may not be combined. Code 34130 (stereo-tactic biopsy). Add procedural code 80610 (cutting needle) or 34150 (mammotome) Code 34205 (U/S FNA) includes the procedural code (may not be combined with 34150). The discovery guidelines are: If the procedure is performed by the radiologist, it should be claimed using codes: o 34150 X-ray mammotome hand held biopsy apparatus AND o 34130 X-ray stereotactic mammography –biopsy OR o 34205 Ultrasound guided aspiration FNA/localization of breast

entries.question: What are the correct billing codes and rates for Cone Beam CT. (Question linked to code 13310-CT of the paranasal sinuses, two planes, limited study)

entries.question: What are the correct billing codes for a radiologist performing ultrasound in theatre? (Question linked to code 00230-Ultrasound guidance)

entries.question: What are the correct codes and practice used for the following: CT perfusion studies of the brain MRI perfusion studies o the brain (Question linked to code 10325-CT brain pre and post contrast for perfusion studies)

entries.question: What are the correct codes for MRI Prostate and biopsy of the prostate? Would they do the biopsy under U/S, CT or MRI guidance
entries.answer: Trans-rectal prostate biopsy is done using ultrasound guidance Trans perineal biopsy (less common) is done using CT or MRI guidance. A cutting needle is usually used rather than FNA If in the case of MRI guidance, 43400 would be appropriate. Diagnostic work up, pre biopsy is a different issue and not part of this question? (Question linked to code 43400-MR study of pelvic reproductive organs - limited study) 43400

entries.question: What are the indication for billing 70240 rather than 70230
entries.answer: 70230 are used for diagnosis of DVT, usually done with patient supine. 70240 is a more complex study which assesses the patency of the deep system and the competency of the valves between deep and superficial. Needs patient to be standing for part of exam. These are used prior to surgery for varicose veins, when you need to not only identify the superficial veins but make sure the deep are patent.

entries.question: What are the indications for billing 70240 rather than 70230 (and vice versa)? (Question linked to code 70230-Ultrasound peripheral venous system lower limbs including pulse and colour doppler for deep vein thrombosis)
entries.answer: 70230 are used for diagnosis of DVT, usually done with patient supine. 70240 is a more complex study which assesses the patency of the deep system and the competency of the valves between deep and superficial. Needs patient to be standing for part of exam. These are used prior to surgery for varicose veins, when you need to not only identify the superficial veins but make sure the deep are patent.

entries.question: What are the rules concerning the use of MRI brain and MRI IAM codes in combination. (Question linked to code 10420-MR of the brain with contrast)

entries.question: What are the rules concerning the use of MRI brain and MRI IAM codes in combination. (Question linked to code 10430-MR of the brain pre and post contrast)

entries.question: What are the rules concerning the use of MRI brain and MRI IAM codes in combination? (Question linked to code 10410-MR of the brain uncontrasted)

entries.question: What are the rules concerning the use of tariff code 01050. (Question linked to code 01050-Written report on study done elsewhere, short)
entries.answer: Code 01050 is used by a radiologist for the reporting of an imaging examination performed elsewhere by a radiologist or radiographer, when a written report is required. This is intended for occasional use for a second opinion or if the original report is not available. It may not be charged for a verbal report. A new code 01065 (Written report by radiologist on general x-ray studies (xx1xx) performed by an independent radiography practice) 0.598 Units is currently only funded by Discovery Health. (05 March 2022), other schemes should follow and these will be announced as it happens. This code should only be used when funded by the patient's medical scheme. For patients whose Medical Aid schemes do not fund 01065, the code 01050 may be used for routine reporting by a radiologist of images produced by an independent radiographer on condition that the total fee (radiologist and radiographer) does not exceed the fee for the same examination if billed by a specialist radiologist.

entries.question: What are the rules regarding contiguous region MR studies such as 10410 (brain) and 51410 (cervical spine). Should one study be down coded or both charged as complete studies? (Question linked to code 10410-MR of the brain uncontrasted)
entries.answer: Most practices handle these studies as follows: (1) Lumbar and scout of thoracic spine billed as full + limited (most commonly requested)

entries.question: What codes can be charged for an angiogram of the upper arm?
entries.answer: "There are no specific codes for upper limb angiography using fluoroscopy. However, the terms angiography and arteriography are sometimes used interchangeably. Therefore, the appropriate codes are: 60500 Arteriogram of subclavian, upper limb arteries alone, unilateral 60510 Arteriogram of subclavian, upper limb arteries alone, bilateral 60520 Arteriogram of aortic arch, subclavian, upper limb, unilateral 60530 Arteriogram of aortic arch, subclavian, upper limb, bilateral Updated 13/03/2025

entries.question: What codes should be billed if ultrasound guidance is done in the ward for insertion of a central line by a specialist and what would be the difference in billing if a radiologist is doing the procedure?
entries.answer: In the ward your coding looks correct except that codes 00210 and 00230 would be better than 00250. Our previous recommendation if the radiologist does it all in the ward is: 1/3 reduction in 80460. Mobile ultrasound can be added but mobile fluoroscopy and attendance in theatre not.

entries.question: What codes to use when an abdominal X-Ray is requested urgently for an acute abdomen or acute abdominal pain? (Question linked to code 40100-X-ray of the abdomen)
entries.answer: When an abdominal X-Ray is requested urgently for an acute abdomen or acute abdominal pain then an Erect and Supine (40105) is appropriate unless the clinical history is specifically recorded as renal stones or faecal loading or similar, in which case 40100 should be used.

entries.question: What is the alternative tariff code for CT coronary if a patient has a high calcium score. (Question linked to code 33300-CT anatomical/functional study of the heart)
entries.answer: The two codes are: 33300 CT anatomical/functional study of the heart 33310 CT angiography of heart vessels If a calcium score is done as an isolated procedure either by design or because a CT angio cannot be done due to excess calcium, 33300 should be used. If a coronary angio gram is done 33310 is used with or without a calcium score. 33300 and 33310 should not be combined.

entries.question: What is the appropriate billing for an MR arthrogram of the shoulder? (Question linked to code 61170-X-ray guidance and introduction of contrast into shoulder joint only)
entries.answer: MRI of the right or left shoulder - 61405 or 61400 plus 61170 - guidance and introcduction of contrast into shoulder joint plus consumables

entries.question: What is the appropriate billing for breast biopsy and localisation. (Question linked to code 34110-X-ray mammography study for localisation)
entries.answer: The rules cover these: Codes 34110 (localization), 34120 (stereo-tactic localization) and 34130 (stereo-tactic biopsy) may not be combined. Code 34130 (stereo-tactic biopsy). Add procedural code 80610 (cutting needle) or 34150 (mammotome) Code 34205 (U/S FNA) includes the procedural code (may not be combined with 34150). The Discovery guidelines are: If the procedure is performed by the radiologist, it should be claimed using codes: o 34150 X-ray mammotome hand held biopsy apparatus AND o 34130 X-ray stereotactic mammography –biopsy OR o 34205 Ultrasound guided aspiration FNA/localization of breast

entries.question: What is the appropriate billing for combined chest and abdominal radiographs? (Question linked to code 30110-X-ray of the chest two views, PA and lateral)
entries.answer: As with most these issues this relies on clinical judgement and an assessment of the diagnostic information required. If the problem is predominantly an abdominal one we would support the routine addition of a PA chest with the erect and supine abdomen to exclude perforation or obstruction. We would not support a PA and lateral chest with every erect and supine abdomen. If you clinically suspect either faecal loading or renal calculi a single AP abdomen should be sufficient. In the case where, in addition to abdominal symptoms, the patient has chest symptoms we would support the addition of a PA and lateral chest.

entries.question: What is the appropriate billing in a case where CT brain and CT facial bones were requested and separate reports were given. (Question linked to code 10300-CT Brain uncontrasted)
entries.answer: I believe the codes 10300 and 11300 below are appropriate: 10300 CT Brain uncontrasted 22.650 11300 CT of the facial bones 20.960

entries.question: What is the appropriate billing in a case where CT brain and CT facial bones were requested and separate reports were given. (Question linked to code 11300-CT Brain uncontrasted)
entries.answer: I believe the codes 10300 and 11300 below are appropriate: 10300 CT Brain uncontrasted 22.650 11300 CT of the facial bones 20.960

entries.question: What is the appropriate tariff code for checking position of PEG tube (eg. GG study via PEG requested)?

entries.question: What is the appropriate tariff code for port insertion and can 00540 or 00550 be billed in combination whether performed in providers radiology suite or not.

entries.question: What is the correct billing code fo a nerve block of head and neck? (Question linked to code 81660-Nerve block, head and neck, per level)

entries.question: What is the correct billing code for a 4D pregnancy scan. (Question linked to code 43270-Ultrasound study of the pregnant uterus, third trimester, first visit)
entries.answer: The codes used by specialist radiologists are: 43270 Ultrasound study of the pregnant uterus, third trimester, first visit 43273 Ultrasound study of the pregnant uterus, third trimester, follow-up visit 43277 Ultrasound study of the pregnant uterus, multiple gestation, second or third trimester, first visit There is no additional or unique code for 4D scans and the RSSA would not support one. 4D scans add nothing to the management of the mother or foetus and are usually done for social indications and unsupportable reasons such as 'allowing the family to bond with a 3D image'

entries.question: What is the correcting coding for multiphasic CT evaluation of renal tract as requested by urologists. (Question linked to code 40313-CT study of the abdomen pre and post contrast)
entries.answer: The post contrast studies can usually be done to show two phases with one scan by splitting the contrast injection, the CT IVU. With correct timing you should be able to get a late arterial/venous and excretory phase on one run. So if the patient has not had a pre contrast scan: 40313 CT study of the abdomen pre and post contrast is used and if the patient has had a pre contrast scan as a CT renal colic scan: 40310 CT of the abdomen with contrast.

entries.question: What is the definition or guideline for a limited sinus study and what would the number of images be. (Question linked to code 13310-CT of the paranasal sinuses, two planes, limited study)
entries.answer: The problem is that, with modern technology, the scan done for limited and full is the same, a block acquisition. The tariff codes were designed for when each slice had to be set up and done individually. So the difference now is not the number of slices but the scope of the radiologists report combined with the request from the clinician. Complicating this if you give a limited report, for example just on mucosal thickening as an indication of sinusitis, you could be held liable for not reporting other pathology. I can’t give hard and fast rules and I think that this is something that you should discuss with your referring doctors, along the lines of your guidelines, different indications for limited and full as you have suggested and reduced number of images presented for limited. Whether it is 5, 12 or twenty is up to you.

entries.question: What is the difference between a normal Doppler and colour Doppler and are different codes used for adults and children. (Question linked to code 33200-Ultrasound study of the heart, including Doppler)
entries.answer: There are basically four types of Doppler: 1. Continuous wave where the sound rather than the image is used. 2. Duplex Doppler, used both to image and assess waveform 3. Colour Doppler which overlays directional colour on to duplex. 4. Power Doppler which is a special form of non-directional colour Doppler. Top end ultrasound machines have had all of the above for twenty years and they are used as needed, by the flick of a switch by the operator.

entries.question: What is the difference between the following codes? 00230, 80605 and 34205 (Question linked to code 00230-Ultrasound guidance)
entries.answer: For breast work Fine Needle Aspiration (FNA) is billed as 34205 which is a composite fee for guidance and billing. FNA any other region except breast is billed as 00230 and 80605 Core biopsy of breast or any region is billed 00230 and 80610

entries.question: What is the maximum use of the code 80605 during one sitting? (Question linked to code 80605-Fine needle aspiration biopsy, any region)
entries.answer: It is recommended that several specimens are obtained in any tissue sampling or biopsy procedure. The diagnostic accuracy increases with up to 4-5 samples. If several passes were made at a lesion, i.e. the needle is inserted through the same region of the skin, this counts as one biopsy procedure. If a biopsy is taken from another site from requiring separate anesthesia of the skin this can be counted as a separate procedure and charged again.

entries.question: What is the tariff code and price or nappi code for STERILE TRAY?
entries.answer: 1. Sterile trays are billed under the consumable code 00090 00090 Consumables used in radiology procedures 2. Suggest the practice confirm the NAPPI code with the supplier of the sterile tray – there are various suppliers of sterile trays and prices differ (the NAPPI code will either be on the purchase invoice or on the packaging) 3. The practice then needs to do a lookup against the NAPPI price file (MedPrax, Medikredit, etc) they are using in their PMA system (once the NAPPI code has been confirmed as per (2) above.

entries.question: What is the tariff code for the following: Loopogram (Question linked to code 40160-X-ray large bowel enema single contrast)
entries.answer: Loopogram – suggest - 40160 - X-ray large bowel enema single contrast enema

entries.question: What is your advice on IVP vs. CT scan for renal stones due to the fact that GP's cannot request a CT scan. (Question linked to code 42300-CT of the renal tract for a stone)
entries.answer: The gold standard for the detection and exclusion of renal stones is non contrast CT. There is no indication for doing an IVU for stones when CT is available. The approach should be; 1. Ensure through CME etc that your supporters are aware of current practice. 2. In the meantime suggest that any potential examination for confirmation or exclusion of stones be discussed with an urologist and the urologist's name, with his or her permission, be used for the referral. 3. We will write to all the medical aids to suggest that those schemes that do not allow direct GP referral and funding of non contrast CT should do so both from a best practice and cost point of view. We will feed back with responses.

entries.question: What is your definition or guideline for a limited sinuses study – is limiting the scan to 12 - 20 images acceptable?
entries.answer: The problem is that, with modern technology, the scan done for limited and full is the same, a block acquisition. The tariff codes were designed for when each slice had to be set up and done individually. So the difference now is not the number of slices but the scope of the radiologists report combined with the request from the clinician. Complicating this if you give a limited report, for example just on mucosa thickening as an indication of sinusitis, you could be held liable for not reporting other pathology. I can’t give hard and fast rules and I think that this is something that you should discuss with your referring doctors, along the lines of your guidelines, different indications for limited and full as you have suggested and reduced number of images presented for limited. Whether it is 5, 12 or twenty is up to you.

entries.answer: Please refer to latest RSSA Stroke Imaging Guidelines.

entries.question: What is your recommendation on 42155 being billed although images were not available at time of reporting, but a second formal report, stating the initial date, gets done when images are available.
entries.answer: It is appropiate to bill 42155 if a report is issued on a pyelogram images.

entries.question: What radiology tariff codes may be used for the MUGA scan requests? (Question linked to code 33905-Nuclear Medicine study - Cardiac blood pool imaging, ejection fraction plus wall motion single study)

entries.question: What the appropriate tariff code is for port insertion. Confirm that 00540 or 00550 may not be billed in combination whether performed in the providers radiology suite or not. (Question linked to code 00540-Digital monoplane screening table)
entries.answer: 87653 Percutaneous tunnelled / subcutaneous arterial or venous central or other line insertion No, only one of the machine codes 00510 - 00560 may be used per procedure / patient visit.

entries.question: What would be standard protocol of views for C-spine and L-spine for "back ache". (Question linked to code 53120-X-ray of the lumbar spine, more than two views)
entries.answer: AP and lateral is sufficient for non referred, non traumatic spinal pain in the first instance. There is no indication for routine oblique views.

entries.question: What would be the appropriate billing for a Triple Rule Out Scan using codes 32305, 33310 and 30360. (Question linked to code 32305-CT angiography of the thoracic and abdominal aorta and branches)
entries.answer: Suggest billing: 33310 CT angiography of heart vessels 30300 CT of the chest, limited study

entries.question: What would be the appropriate billing for a Triple Rule Out Scan using codes 32305, 33310 and 30360. (Question linked to code 33310-CT angiography of heart vessels)
entries.answer: Suggest billing: 33310 CT angiography of heart vessels 30300 CT of the chest, limited study

entries.question: What would be the appropriate billing if a specialist makes use of radiology's screening room to perform a procedure, eg. HSG. (Question linked to code 43120-X-ray hystero-salpingography)
entries.answer: If an HSG is done in the radiologist's rooms, the radiologist supplies equipment and radiographer. The radiologist may do the cervical catheterisation and introduce contrast, or this may be done by the gynaecologist. In either case the radiologist will report on the study. One or other of these codes applicable: 43120 X-ray hystero-salpingography (introduction of contrast done by gynae) 43130 X-ray hystero-salpingography with introduction of contrast (introduction of contrast done by radiologist) The same logic applies to other procedures.

entries.question: What would be the appropriate billing where scan gets done by one practice and reports gets done by another practice due to the fact that a radiologist specialized in the specific field is not available.
entries.answer: It is important to note that the report done by the second practice is not a second opinion and therefor code 01055 cannnot be billed as an initial report was not given in the first place. Stating that the first pracxtice has reviewed the images is not an opinion as neither findings nor conclusion are documented. The first practice is therefore sending the case for a first opinion. Whatever fee is charged by the second practice is an arrangement between the two practices and should not involve either the patient or the medical scheme in any additional cost. The first practice is charging for the study and it is their responsibility to get it reported. Even if the first practice had issued a report we do not support a fee for a second opinion under these circumstances

entries.question: What would be the appropriate code to use (40333 or 40340) in a patient with a history of abdominal pain. (Question linked to code 40333-CT of the abdomen and pelvis with contrast)
entries.answer: Under the circumstances with no history of haematuria and no specific requests to exclude pancreatic pathology it is appropriate that a contrasted study is performed. 40340 is not indicated and our recommendation for general abdominal screening for non-specific pain would be: 40333: CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast.

entries.question: What would be the appropriate code to use (40333 or 40340) in a patient with a history of abdominal pain. (Question linked to code 40340-CT triphasic study of the liver, abdomen and pelvis pre and post contrast)
entries.answer: Under the circumstances with no history of haematuria and no specific requests to exclude pancreatic pathology it is appropriate that a contrasted study is performed. 40340 is not indicated and our recommendation for general abdominal screening for non-specific pain would be: 40333: CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast.

entries.question: What would be the correct billing code/s if both hips were requested. (Question linked to code 56120-X-ray pelvis and hips)
entries.answer: 56120 X-ray pelvis and hips following general principle that one code should be used when possible. ------------------------------- 56100 X-ray of the left hip 56110 X-ray of the right hip Are used when one or other is done.

entries.question: What would be the correct billing for reporting done on X-rays by a radiographer that owns her own practice. (Question linked to code 01065-0)
entries.answer: Radiologists and radiographers can work in association either in an employer/employee relationship or as partners in a group practice. Fee splitting is not as issue as the Ethical Rules only prohibit this if the fees are shared with a person who is not registered with the HPCSA or has not taken a part in the service. The latter prevents kick-backs to another professional for referral. Radiologists and radiographers both contribute to the service and fees may be shared. Radiologists and radiographers can form a group practice and bill specialist radiology rates on condition that every case is reported by a radiologist. Radiologists can theoretically bill separately for their services on the same patient. There is a new code: (01065 – Written report for general x-ray studies performed by an independent radiography practice) The principal of using this code has been accepted by Discovery Health which has allocated a benefit to it.

entries.question: What would be the correct billing for the following exams: CT brain and sinuses CT brain and facial bones CT brain and facial bones including mandible (Question linked to code 10300-CT Brain uncontrasted)
entries.answer: A reasonable approach is tabulated below: the coding is related to area examined. CT Brain 10300 CT Brain and sinuses 10300 plus 13320 or 13300 CT Brain and facial bones 10300 plus 11300 CT Brain and facial bones including mandible 10300 plus 11300 CT Brain and cervical spine (often for trauma) 10300 plus 51320

entries.question: What would be the correct billing if request is for X-rays of both hips. (Question linked to code 56100-X-ray of the left hip)
entries.answer: 56120 X-ray pelvis and hips following general principle that one code should be used when possible. 56100 X-ray of the left hip 56110 X-ray of the right hip Are used when one or other is done.

entries.question: When CT brain (10300), CT facial bones (11300), and CT C-spine are scanned/reported separately, should CT facial bones (11300) be removed and not charged separately due to overlap with CT brain and CT C-spine?
entries.answer: When CT brain, CT facial bones and CT C-spine are billed together, it would not be unreasonable to bill CT brain full (10300), CT facial bones full (11300), and C-spine limited (51300).

entries.question: When is it appropriate to charge for a triphasic scan (40340). (Question linked to code 40340-CT triphasic study of the liver, abdomen and pelvis pre and post contrast)
entries.answer: Triple phase is when the patient is scanned three times post contrast injection. The indications for the triple phase are guideline dependent and not request dependent. The radiologists should assess the need for triple phase bearing in mind current international best practice and the requirement to keep radiation to as low a level as possible.
