Is this just an undeerstanding, or are there actual laws prohibiting it? I would appreciate your input on the above matters.
entries.answer: In respect to the your questions, initial response: Dr X wants to buy into an existing partnership, but doesn't have the funds to do so….A practitioner can source funds from anywhere. The sensible approach would be to cede the rights to equipment purchased to the lender as collateral but not any share of the practice or its income. Drs A, B and C wants to start a new practice but don't have the money to purchase the equipment…. Will be covered in opinion.

entries.question: Could you possibly assist? I have been asked to look into this but have been unable to find any applicable information. We have had to employ an “Authorizations Clerk” who is employed to authorize all CT and MRI scans. Is there any tariff code available to Radiologists to charge for the service whereby we are obtaining authorization on behalf of the patient?
entries.answer: There is no code for this. Part of global practice expense.

entries.question: Ek wil graag by jou hoor of is jul dalk bewys van ‘n kode wat deur die radioloo gebruik kan word wanneer ons ‘n drip vir ‘n pasient moet opsit? Ek weet die kode 0206 word gewoonlik gebruik deur die verwysende dr’s? Ek glo ons as radioloo sal ‘n soortgelyke 5 kode nommer he?
entries.answer: The code 0206 is a four digit code and should not be used by radiologists. There is no radiology code for putting up a drip. Obtaining intravenous access for injection of contrast is part of the procedure and does not attract a separate code or get billed separately. There are codes for insertion of central lines but that is a different issue.

entries.question: I trust that you are well. Please assist me in the following: I have a referring Doctor who refuses that we give examinations/results to the patient. We have to deliver the results sealed to his Practice. Some of his patients are requesting the results for a second opinion. He refuses to give it to them – our Practice are now issuing duplicates for these patients. Is this acceptable as the patient pays our account? Can the referring Doctor force us not to give any results to the patient? Thank you for the assistance in this sensitive matter.
entries.answer: I have to make a couple of assumptions in the answer as I am not sure how you are practicing. I am not sure whether you give patients reports for their own records or you are using the patient as a delivery mechanism. The patient has the absolute right to access to his or her own report. The RSSA’s recommendation is that unsealed reports should not be given to patients routinely but must be given to patients when requested. The patient is referred to you by a referring doctor and it is not unreasonable that the doctor sees the reports first , if that is what he or she wants, as long as the results of the examination are given to the patient in a reasonable time. The are many cases, such as a first cancer diagnosis, where this approach is advisable. The nuance is about timing. However, if the referring doctor wants all the reports sent to him, the referring doctor must be prepared to give a copy of the report to the patient if requested or forward for second opinions. I am not sure whether your reports are on paper or analogue but if the former you could send the referring doctor two copies and request him/her to give one to the patient if requested. If digital the referrer must be prepared to print or further distribute the report to the patient. The referring doctor cannot prevent you from giving results to patients when requested. I think that one of your radiologists should discuss this with the referring doctor and come to a solution that suits both parties.

entries.question: Please confirm if Medical aids should pay for x-rays referred by a physiotherapist.
entries.answer: In general yes. A radiologist may accept referrals from any practitioner registered with the HPCSA. Medical schemes have rules which determine who may refer what. For example most schemes will not allow GPS to refer CT and MRI. The reasonable expectation is that a physiotherapist should be allowed to refer plain film X-rays and ultrasound.

entries.question: Kan julle asb net weer vir ons praktyk duidelikheid gee tot wanneer ons swangerskaps sonars mag doen? Ons doen slegs eerste trimester swangerskappe vir gestasie bepaling by ons praktyk, maar is nie seker of ons kan sonar doen tot einde 12 weke of einde 13 weke gestasie nie. Wat is die RSSA se beleid?
entries.answer: The RSSA position paper on this is attached, along with a communication from MPS and a suggested letter to referring doctors. Unless you have full indemnity from MPS, our recommendations, in summary, are: 1. You do not do any obstetric ultrasound examination for patients with clinical dates greater than 13 weeks, for any clinical indication. 2. You may confirm the pregnancy, report on whether or not the pregnancy is intra- or extra-uterine, estimate the gestational age and comment on the pelvic organs. 3. You should make the patient aware before the examination that no attempt will be made to assess foetal anatomy or foetal abnormality. A comment to this effect should be put in the report. Recommended text: 'First trimester scan, restricted to pregnancy confirmation, location and estimation of gestational age, patient advised that she should be referred for foetal assessment at the appropriate time.’ 4. A disclaimer that you will not be liable for undetected foetal abnormalities will not absolve you from liability and is not recommended. 5. You should not do nuchal thickness measurements or analysis. 6. If the ultrasound dates are a few days over the thirteen week limit, report as above but make it clear that the exam was done either because the dates were unknown or because the clinical dates were expected to be less than 13 weeks. 7. If there is a mismatch between the clinical dates and ultrasound dates and the ultrasound dates are well over 13 weeks abandon the scan, inform the patient and refer her back to her doctor or someone with appropriate indemnity.

entries.question: I’m preparing a presentation on prostate cancer and I’m trying to establish how many Linear Accelerators there are in South Africa. Can you please direct me to where I can obtain this information?
entries.answer: https://sites.google.com/site/radiationcontroldoh/

entries.question: Can a radiographer by and own a CT and get a licence for it?
entries.answer: Very much a grey area. The HPCSA guidelines from the Medical and Dental Board say any registered person may own any equipment. We have opposed this. Radiation control will not licence a CT to a radiographer and should not licence a CT to anyone unless a radiologist takes responsibility for it. With Radiation Control coming under PRAHSA we are not sure what will happen.

entries.question: I need your assistance again today. Will you be able to confirm if specialists of discipline 40, are allowed to perform MRI & CT scans? And if so, is there a selected list?
entries.answer: There are codes in the old SAMA guide (section 20) as attached for radiation oncologists to bill for planning using MR and CT. The imaging costs are not included and should be charged by the owner of the CT or MRI.

entries.question: We discussed the "Scope of radiographer" document at our recent SASNM meeting. (Unfortunately the document doesnt want to upload, but Im sure you have it) The main issue that came up was if radiographers are legally allowed to administer pharmaceutical products such as Lasix in the absence of a doctor. In the document it refers to "collaboration with a nuclear medicine physician for the medical use of .....administration of pharmaceuticals .... as required for the procedures of nuclear medicine" (p28, point 5) which is a bit vague and open for interpretation. Our radiographer representative raised concerns conveyed to her by our members that some radiographers in private practice are requested to administer Persantin (vasodilator used in cardiac stress test) or even pediatric sedation at times, without any doctor being present on site. I spoke to legal advisors from SAMA and their interpretation of the document is that administration of pharmaceuticals is NOT in the scope of practice for a radiographer, and that the wording "collaboration" implies a doctor being present in person. What is the RSSA interpretation of this, as the same sentence is also used under diagnostic, diagnostic ultrasound as well as radiation therapy? Is there a need to be more specific about this matter?
entries.answer: The wording in the section which applies to radiology is “medicine control: collaboration with a radiologist or medical practitioner in the control and administration of contrast media or medicines as required for such diagnostic procedures”. We were happy with this and did not make any specific comment. Our interpretation of this and that of the Medical Protection Society is that radiographers may only administer contrast material or medicines when prescribed by a radiologist and when either a radiologist or medical practitioner is in close proximity to the radiology department, is aware of the procedure and takes responsibility for the management of any potential side effects.

entries.question: We have a satellite branch in Standerton with only one radiographer there. We used to have a locum radiographer to assist ours in times when she is on leave, etc. Our locum radiographer retired now. We did an interview with a community service diagnostic radiographer that is available on certain days to assist with the locum work. Are they allowed to work in private practice during their community service year?
entries.answer: No, community service is a limited and transitional registration to work in the public sector. They cannot work in either the public service other than in a comm serv job, or in the private sector without being registered in the ‘independent practice category.

entries.question: While I am watching you on you tube I just wanted to find out from you whether Radiology has officially been declared as an essential service for purposes of strikes, etc. – a question that was posed to us by our Labour Lawyer yesterday? To us it is obvious especially for branches situated within hospitals but is it official?
entries.answer: On the surface it seems simple but looking more deeply it becomes complicated without a clear answer. The definition of “essential services” in the Labour Relations Act is as follows: “essential service” means— (a) a service the interruption of which endangers the life, personal safety or health of the whole or any part of the population; (b) the Parliamentary service; (c) the South African Police Services The following medical related services in the private sector have been declared essential: “5. It has designated as essential services the following services provided by the private sector which are funded by the public sector: (a) Emergency health services and the provision of emergency health facilities to the community or part thereof; (b) nursing; and (c) medical and paramedical services. From the above it appears that the private sector is excluded unless the private sector is providing services funded by the public sector. But here is the conundrum: Is a practice providing RAF and COID services providing services funded by the public sector? Are providers providing services to GEMs providing services for the public sector? Our legal advisors don’t have the answer to this. Further the private sector can apply to have a service classified as an essential service. Some examples of essential services are attached.are attached.

entries.question: I am doing some internal Deloitte research (not to be published) around regulation surrounding radiology in South Africa. A key outstanding piece of information we are looking for is about service provider regulations for radiology services in South Africa. Are hospital groups allowed to run their own radiology businesses / services or does this always have to be contracted to an external service provider (such as your members). Please could you assist by providing guidance on the above?
entries.answer: Under current HPCSA regulations and Ethical Rules radiologists cannot work in association with third parties not registered as health professionals. This precludes a private hospital from owning a radiology department and employing radiologists.

entries.question: I had recently had a MRI done, my medical scheme short paid me, therefore there is an amount due by me, but the account arrives every month with interest included. How legal is this? They are not a Registered Financial Services Provider, which will give them the facility to charge interest.
entries.answer: 1. Financial Advisory and Intermediary Services Act (FAIS Act). a. Financial service providers are licenced under the FAIS Act. b. To be licensed they must provide advice or an intermediary service in respect of a financial product. c. A practitioner is not a financial service provider. 2. National Credit Act a. With reference to the National Credit Act, the addition of interest to an account, will qualify as an “incidental credit agreement”, which is defined as follows: “incidental credit agreement” means an agreement, irrespective of its form, in terms of which an account was tendered for goods or services that have been provided to the consumer, or goods or services that are to be provided to a consumer over a period of time and either or both of the following conditions apply— (a) a fee, charge or interest became payable when payment of an amount charged in terms of that account was not made on or before a determined period or date; or (b) two prices were quoted for settlement of the account, the lower price being applicable if the account is paid on or before a determined date, and the higher price being applicable due to the account not having been paid by that date. From the above our conclusion is: a. A practitioner is not a financial service provider. b. A practitioner who charges interest as contemplated in the National Credit Act is a credit provider in respect of an incidental credit agreement, but does not need to register as a credit provider under this Act. The practitioner must still comply with the provisions of this Act. c. If the radiology practice complies with the provisions of the Act it may add interest to outstanding accounts.

entries.question: This matter could have been dealt with in past communication – apologies in advance We have had a couple of recent “incidents”. Our protocol regarding report distribution has always been that the referring clinician has referred the patient to our practice. The radiology report (as well as images where applicable) are then send, delivered, emailed or available via PACS to the referring clinician. The report could be given to the patient to take back to the referring clinician. This will be done in a sealed envelope. One of the reasons have always been that the referring clinician can explain any findings to the patient, where applicable. The couple of recent incidents we have had, is where patients have insisted that they get a copy of the report, as they are paying for the service (self or funder), they are entitled to a copy. Our frontline staff have to answer these questions, and sometimes insults and threats. They have even been threatened with legal action. What is the legal and/or ethical standing on this? Who is the owner, or owners of the report? Who are all allowed to receive the report, or copies of it? Who must receive the report, and who not? Etc.?
entries.answer: I had a similar query about ownership of reports and images from Mediclinic very recently and the legal situation is: 'The answer is a generic one and applies to health records as a whole both in SA and internationally. The health record itself belongs to the organisation or individual which produces it, hospital, clinician, radiologist etc. This would include both the radiology images and the radiology report. If radiology images and reports are stored on a third party system the images and reports still belong to the producer, unless an agreement is in place to transfer ownership (with or without a financial consideration) The patient and the referring doctor have the right of ownership of the contents of the medical report so in radiology have the right to copies of the images and reports. In modern practice access to images on a PACS or RIS would be enough for the referring doctor. There is no obligation for the radiologist to routinely provide the patient with copies of images and reports but should do so on request.' So the way to approach this is to advise patients that you would prefer the results to be explained to the patient by the referring clinician but not prevent them asking for a radiologist to explain them or give them a copy of the report should they wish that. Please let me know if there is anything further you need.

entries.question: In terms of the POPI Act, are we allowed to send images to a contracted third party for reporting? Will we be infringing on the POPI Act? Would we need to obtain special consent from the patients?
entries.answer: The opinion we have received is as follows: 'On the information available, it looks as if the contracted third party will be an “operator” for the specific practice. The POPI Act contains requirements for arrangements with operators. There must be a written contract, which must comply with certain specified requirements. In essence, the required security measures must be in place. It is also advisable that the practice make reference to such an out-sourced relationship in its patient documentation (e.g. terms and conditions of treatment, privacy policy, etc.). The fact that a third party receives the information and reports on it, must also be communicated to patients in terms of section 18 of POPI. Special consent is therefore not necessary. The relevant sections of the Act, if the third party is in SA are copied below. If the third party is outside SA additional sections of the Act will apply.' 1. “operator” means a person who processes personal information for a responsible party in terms of a contract or mandate, without coming under the direct authority of that party. 2. Section 20. Information processed by operator or person acting under authority An operator or anyone processing personal information on behalf of a responsible party or an operator, must— (a) process such information only with the knowledge or authorisation of the responsible party; and (b) treat personal information which comes to their knowledge as confidential and must not disclose it, unless required by law or in the course of the proper performance of their duties. 3. Section 21. Security measures regarding information processed by operator (1) A responsible party must, in terms of a written contract between the responsible party and the operator, ensure that the operator which processes personal information for the responsible party establishes and maintains the security measures referred to in section 19. (2) The operator must notify the responsible party immediately where there are reasonable grounds to believe that the personal information of a data subject has been accessed or acquired by any unauthorised person. 4. Section 19. Security measures on integrity and confidentiality of personal information (1) A responsible party must secure the integrity and confidentiality of personal information in its possession or under its control by taking appropriate, reasonable technical and organisational measures to prevent— (a) loss of, damage to or unauthorised destruction of personal information; and (b) unlawful access to or processing of personal information. (2) In order to give effect to subsection (1), the responsible party must take reasonable measures to— (a) identify all reasonably foreseeable internal and external risks to personal information in its possession or under its control; (b) establish and maintain appropriate safeguards against the risks identified; (c) regularly verify that the safeguards are effectively implemented; and (d) ensure that the safeguards are continually updated in response to new risks or deficiencies in previously implemented safeguards. (3) The responsible party must have due regard to generally accepted information security practices and procedures which may apply to it generally or be required in terms of specific industry or professional rules and regulations. 5. Section 18. Notification to data subject when collecting personal information (1) If personal information is collected, the responsible party must take reasonably practicable steps to ensure that the data subject is aware of— (a) the information being collected and where the information is not collected from the data subject, the source from which it is collected; (b) the name and address of the responsible party; (c) the purpose for which the information is being collected; (d) whether or not the supply of the information by that data subject is voluntary or mandatory; (e) the consequences of failure to provide the information; (f) any particular law authorising or requiring the collection of the information; (g) the fact that, where applicable, the responsible party intends to transfer the information to a third country or international organisation and the level of protection afforded to the information by that third country or international organisation; (h) any further information such as the— (i) recipient or category of recipients of the information; (ii) nature or category of the information; (iii) existence of the right of access to and the right to rectify the information collected; (iv) the existence of the right to object to the processing of personal information as referred to in section 11 (3); and (v) right to lodge a complaint to the Information Regulator and the contact details of the Information Regulator, which is necessary, having regard to the specific circumstances in which the information is or is not to be processed, to enable processing in respect of the data subject to be reasonable. (2) The steps referred to in subsection (1) must be taken— (a) if the personal information is collected directly from the data subject, before the information is collected, unless the data subject is already aware of the information referred to in that subsection; or (b) in any other case, before the information is collected or as soon as reasonably practicable after it has been collected. (3) A responsible party that has previously taken the steps referred to in subsection (1) complies with subsection (1) in relation to the subsequent collection from the data subject of the same information or information of the same kind if the purpose of collection of the information remains the same. (4) It is not necessary for a responsible party to comply with subsection (1) if— (a) the data subject or a competent person where the data subject is a child has provided consent for the non-compliance; (b) non-compliance would not prejudice the legitimate interests of the data subject as set out in terms of this Act; (c) non-compliance is necessary— (i) to avoid prejudice to the maintenance of the law by any public body, including the prevention, detection, investigation, prosecution and punishment of offences; (ii) to comply with an obligation imposed by law or to enforce legislation concerning the collection of revenue as defined in section 1 of the South African Revenue Service Act, 1997 (Act No. 34 of 1997); (iii) for the conduct of proceedings in any court or tribunal that have been commenced or are reasonably contemplated; or (iv) in the interests of national security; (d) compliance would prejudice a lawful purpose of the collection; (e) compliance is not reasonably practicable in the circumstances of the particular case; or (f) the information will— (i) not be used in a form in which the data subject may be identified; or (ii) be used for historical, statistical or research purposes. (Similar information to what is required by section 18 will also go into the PAIA Manual of the practice).

entries.question: Our practice needs some help/advice please. The third party claims are bringing a lot of extra admin/paperwork. We do not accept 3rd party claims (patients have to pay cash or claim from the medical aid), but we do assist the attorneys later on, by providing reports and invoices on request. Is there a fee/tariff that we can charge for this? How does other practices handle this?
entries.answer: These accounts are not usually covered by medical aids and should be rendered at a rate you think appropriate. We cannot advise on that. Your client in most cases is the attorneys requesting the examination. Practices either make arrangements for pre- or guaranteed payment from the attorneys before doing these cases. The RSSA does not support work being done on a contingency basis when the radiologist is only paid when and if the case is settled. You have provided a professional service irrespective of the outcome of the case.

entries.question: Reduced Membership Subscriptions for full-time assistants in private practice
entries.answer: Thank you for you enquiry about reduced membership subscriptions for full-time assistants in private practice. We have had this enquiry several times before. The problem is that practice structures vary with some being mainly partners and other mainly assistants. Because of this we have had to keep a standard subscription for private practice irrespective of whether the radiologist is a partner or assistant. The way most practices manage this is that the practice pays all subscriptions as a practice expense rather than making it part of the assistant’s package or making the assistant liable for its payment. We cannot dictation this but it may be an idea for you to have a discussion with your managing partner?

entries.question: Is there any legal or medical requirement for private patients to obtain a referral note before any x-ray procedure may be performed? I know that it is a requirement by medical aid funds but that relates to payments more than anything else.
entries.answer: It is recommended in our code of conduct, the main reason being that the patient may not know the appropriate imaging needed. There is, however, no ethical or legal impediment to a patent coming to you without a referral letter. Any patient may consult a specialist without being referred by another healthcare professional. In this instance the responsibility lies with the radiologist to determine the appropriate imaging needed.

entries.question: Our doctors want confirmation if referrals written out by ward sister is acceptable? May she sign on behalf of the dr? Should the referring doctors signature be on the referral letter ?
entries.answer: I asked Esme the question a while ago and received the following: 'There is according to the best of my knowledge no regulation as to who may refer to whom. In my view Ethical Rule 9(2) should be considered. It provides as follows: “9. Covering (2) A practitioner shall help or support only a person registered under the Act, the Pharmacy Act, 1974 (Act No. 53 of 1974), the Nursing Act, 1978 (Act No. 50 of 1978), the Social Service Professions Act, 1978 (Act No. 110 of 1978), the Dental Technicians Act, 1979 (Act No. 19 of 1979), or the Allied Health Professions Act, 1982 (Act No. 63 of 1982), if the professional practice or conduct of such person is legal and within the scope of his or her profession.” In terms of this Rule a practitioner (person registered at the HPCSA) may only help or support practitioners registered under the mentioned acts provided that the conduct of such persons is legal and within their scope of profession.' So from the HPCSA point of view a request form may be signed by a nursing sister or any other registered healthcare worker. The issue is probably more related to the requirement of medical schemes. Scheme rules should be consulted. As long as the form is pp. by the nurse on behalf of the specialist and the specialist takes responsibility for the referral there would not appear to be a problem.

entries.question: We require some clarification on doing screening mammography with out a referral letter or indeed a doctor to then follow up the results. I use an example of a patient walking in off the street requesting a mammogram. You are saying we don't need a referral letter and indeed some or all of the medical aids do not require a referral letter to make payment( I stand to be corrected but at least Discovery definitely does not require a referral letter for payment). If suspicious findings are made which require clinical correlation or follow up, how is the patient supposed to understand this and then themselves find a practitioner to medically interpret the results. If for example the report reads" suspicious calcifications demonstrated which require repeat imaging in 6months" and the patient is ignorant or chooses not to go,where does the responsibility fall? Another example would be a finding of benign fibroadenoma (probable). We may not know the background family history of the patient and may choose sonar follow up where a clinician may want to excise or biopsy knowing the family history. The free screening and payment without a referral note appears to create a grey area and i am unsure of the medico-legal implications.
entries.answer: There is a distinction between having a referral letter and having a doctor to whom the report goes to. If the medical aid requires a referral letter for a screening procedure then the patient must get one anyway. In the case where a referral letter is not required the patient can book for a mammogram without a physical referral letter. There must however be a doctor to whom the report is sent, which will be the patients GP or gynaecologist. If the patient does not have one you should ensure that the report goes to a practitioner who can provide appropriate further referral or treatment. Discuss this with the patient. In the case of suspected pre-malignancy or malignancy it is always preferable to follow up the report with verbal communication with the doctor who will be responsible for further action. The comment above applies, irrespective of age. For a screening procedure a referral letter is not necessary unless the patient’s medical aid needs one. Every patient screened must nominate a doctor to whom the report is sent. It is the radiologist's responsible to ensure that significant results are communicated to a practitioner who will continue care. If a radiologist does a biopsy and there is malignancy he/she is responsible until the result has been definitely received and acknowledged by someone who will continue care.

entries.question: If I can please request your guidance on the following scenarios: 1. GP/specialist refers patient for a scan on to Practice A. Patient decides to go to practice B with referral note from practice A. Can practice B do the scan, or must they direct the patient back to practice A? 2. Specialist A refers patient to another specialist B. Specialist A works with specialist B and is confident in his/her work and diagnosis. Patient decides to go to specialist C. Patient goes back to specialist A with results from specialist C. What can specialist A do in this circumstances?
entries.answer: The relevant sections of the Ethical Rules are: 10. Supersession.—A practitioner shall not supersede or take over a patient from another practitioner if he or she is aware that such patient is in active treatment of another practitioner, unless he or she— • (a) takes reasonable steps to inform the other practitioner that he or she has taken over the patient at such patient’s request; and • (b) establishes from the other practitioner what treatment such patient previously received, especially what medication, if any, was prescribed to such patient and in such case the other practitioner shall be obliged to provide such required information. 11. Impeding a patient.—A practitioner shall not impede a patient, or in the case of a minor, the parent or guardian of such minor, from obtaining the opinion of another practitioner or from being treated by another practitioner. Your two scenarios below are the same except that one involves a specialist and the other a general practitioner. In the case of referral to a radiologist, I do not think that the supersession clause is that relevant as the radiologist is not involved in ‘active treatment’. The clause on ‘is impeding a patient’ is more relevant. The patient has the ultimate choice of who performs treatment. The radiology referral form is provided as a convenience and although usually one will expect the patient to go to the practice providing the form there is no obligation to do so. The decision on which radiologist to use is a decision to be made by the referring doctor and patient and as a general rule the radiology practice should not intervene in this. As a practical rule if a patient arrives at a radiology practice with a referral note from another practice it would be best to discuss this with the patient and ask whether he or she has made the referring doctor aware that he or she has come to another practice. This all assumes that there is no perversity involved in the referrals, if there is that would be an entirely different issue. Unless this is a major problem with significant financial impact, it is probably not worth wasting too much time on it as it is likely to work in both directions! Please let me know if there is anything further you need to know on this?

entries.question: 1. Need to know – do you need a letter from a referring Dr for a bone density scan? 2. Do they need authorisation for this procedure.
entries.answer: 1. For a screening procedure a referral letter is not necessary unless the patient’s medical aid needs one. 2. Every patient screened must nominate a doctor to whom the report is sent. 3. It is the radiologist's responsible to ensure that significant results are communicated to a practitioner who will continue care. 4. Authorization for procedures is plan (scheme) specific – suggest the practice confirm with the schemes.

entries.question: We receive request every now and then from GP doctors requesting for scans. Will you be able to assist us with any documents stating that GP’s are not allowed to sign referral letters for specialised radiology. We want to forward this letter to referring GP’s.
entries.answer: There are no HPCSA or RSSA regulations defining who may refer what. Technically any registered medical practitioner may refer a patient to a radiologist for any study. The issue is that the individual medical scheme rules dictate which referrals are funded. Most schemes will not pay for CT and MRI referral from GPs. Making it more complicated the rules are not generic or uniform and some schemes allow GP referral for high tech for GPS enrolled in networks (e.g Discovery Keycare)

entries.question: Please advise if we can do reporting for a clinic in Swaziland. The Radiographer is an employee of this clinic. The clinic wants us to claim from SA medical aids (tourists from SA). The Radiographer is not registered at HPCSA but the referring practitioner is.
entries.answer: The first is that for teleradiology the RSSA’s and HPCSA’s position is that the reporting radiologist should be registered as specialist radiologist in the country where the patient is and the image is produced. The reporting radiologists should therefore be registered with the Swaziland Medical and Dental Council, irrespective of where the radiologist is doing the reporting. You would have to contact them to confirm but it seems as if South African Medical professionals get automatic registration. Whether they can practice in Swaziland with South African HPCSA registration, without formal SMDC registration, is not clear and you should ask the SMDC this. As long as the radiographer is registered with the SMDC that is OK. Then the referring doctor, radiographer and radiologist will all have Swazi registration. Payment by Medical Schemes will depend on the rules of each scheme, i.e. whether they provide cover for procedures performed in Swaziland. It would be illegal to bill as if the examination had been doe in SA. I hope that this helps. Please get back to me if you need further information.

entries.question: Are we as Practice allowed to give / email a PATIENT his/her report if requested with consent?
entries.answer: The patient have the absolute right to their reports. No consent required from the patient it is theirs anyway.

entries.question: Or are we only allowed to give the report to the referring Doctor?
entries.answer: The case referred to is van der Walt v de Beer. Judgement attached. Lotz was quoted in the judgement saying: ‘An X-ray without radiologist’s report is a time bomb, is a waiting time bomb. There its no exclusion to that rule.’ The judge is just quoting Lotz’s evidence it has no force in itself. We have not been able to establish that it is legally required that all studies are reported but it is good practice and we recommend it,

entries.question: I am currently setting up a clinical trial in South Africa that requires a transvaginal ultrasound to measure endometrial thickness in menopausal women. Can you please confirm if this procedure is within the scope of a radiologist or is this a procedure that can only be done by a gynaecologist?
entries.answer: Please advise that this is very definitely within the scope of a diagnostic radiologist

entries.question: Retention of Radiology Records
entries.answer: RETENTION OF RADIOLOGY RECORDS The question of how long radiology records should be kept was discussed at the recent RSSA council meeting. There is no specific legislation which covers the retention of medical records but it falls under both the Protection of Personal Information Act (POPI) and the recommendations of the HPCSA. There is no specific code of conduct for the medical profession which is applicable. The POPI legislation is about the protection of confidentiality and section 14(1)-(3) of POPI provides as follows: "14. Retention and restriction of records. (1) Subject to subsections (2) and (3), records of personal information must not be retained any longer than is necessary for achieving the purpose for which the information was collected or subsequently processed, unless— (a) retention of the record is required or authorised by law; (b) the responsible party reasonably requires the record for lawful purposes related to its functions or activities; (c) retention of the record is required by a contract between the parties thereto; or (d) the data subject or a competent person where the data subject is a child has consented to the retention of the record. (2) Records of personal information may be retained for periods in excess of those contemplated in subsection (1) for historical, statistical or research purposes if the responsible party has established appropriate safeguards against the records being used for any other purposes. (3) A responsible party that has used a record of personal information of a data subject to make a decision about the data subject, must— (a) retain the record for such period as may be required or prescribed by law or a code of conduct; or (b) if there is no law or code of conduct prescribing a retention period, retain the record for a period which will afford the data subject a reasonable opportunity, taking all considerations relating to the use of the personal information into account, to request access to the record. The Health Professions Council of South Africa (HPCSA) makes the following recommendation: • Records should be kept for at least six years after they become dormant. • The records of minors should be kept until their 21st birthday. • The records of patients who are mentally impaired should be kept until the patient's death. • Records pertaining to illness or accident arising from a person's occupation should be kept for 20 years after treatment has ended. Records kept in provincial hospitals and clinics should only be destroyed with the authorisation of the Deputy Director-General concerned. • Retention periods should be extended if there are reasons for doing so, such as when a patient has been exposed to conditions that might manifest in a slow-developing disease, such as asbestosis. In these circumstances, the HPCSA recommends keeping the records for at least 25 years. • The cost and space implications of keeping records indefinitely must be balanced against the possibility that records will be found useful in the defence of litigation or for academic or research purposes. • Statutory obligations to keep certain types of records for specific periods must be complied with. Both reports and images are classified as records. The RSSA considers 'as long as necessary' as the period recommended by the HPCSA and recommends the following: REPORTS All reports should be stored for the periods recommended by the HPCSA as above. DIGITAL IMAGES Digital images should be stored for the period recommended by the HPCSA as above. ANALOGUE IMAGES If retained by the radiologist, for the periods specified above. If the images are delivered to a referring doctor or given to the patient and none are retained by the radiologist, a signature should be obtained confirming receipt by the doctor or patient and the record of this delivery/receipt be kept for at least six years. It is also recommended that a clause is inserted in the standard terms and conditions form, signed by the patient, specifying the period for which record will be retained.

entries.question: I wish to have clarity on the following: 1. Is there is a legal / competency requirement for Radiologist to retire at the age of 65 years? 2. If not, are Radiologist allowed to practice indefinitely? 3. What are the laws governing the process to allow Radiologist to continue in service beyond 65 years. Is there a test of their skill and fitness to practice?
entries.answer: There is no legal retirement requiring any practitioner to retire at any age in South Africa. Any employer, including the State may make this part of the terms and conditions of employment. Any partnership or Incorporated practice may stipulate a retirement age, that is by negotiation and agreement and usually binding once signed. Internationally the concept of stopping people working due to age is considered discriminatory and ageist but this has to be looked at realistically when safety is concerned, airline pilots etc. In the UK doctors can work beyond 65 but annual appraisal is required. Pension age is different and controlled by legislation in different jurisdictions. If this doesn’t answer your question, please give me more specific details to allow me to assist you.

entries.question: Could you please forward me a copy of the RSSA guidelines (regarding billing rules)?
entries.answer: http://rssa.co.za/clinical-guidelines-and-protocols/blog.html and http://rssa.co.za/billing/nhrpl-and-coida-tariffs.html

entries.question: enquiry about reduced membership subscriptions for full-time assistants in private practice.
entries.answer: We have had this enquiry several times before. The problem is that practice structures vary with some being mainly partners and other mainly assistants. Because of this we have had to keep a standard subscription for private practice irrespective of whether the radiologist is a partner or assistant. The way most practices manage this is that the practice pays all subscriptions as a practice expense rather than making it part of the assistant’s package or making the assistant liable for its payment. We cannot dictation this but it may be an idea for you to have a discussion with your managing partner?

entries.question: Good afternoon I hope your association can assist in this query, or alternatively refer me to the correct bodies. We frequently see medical practitioners claiming code 1241 for looking / interpreting CXR’s taken by radiologists. My understanding of this code, and where it is situated in the gazette, is that it is a RADIOLOGY code to be charged by the radiologist during cardiovascular procedures. I am of the opinion that it should NEVER be chargeable by non-radiologists. Is this interpretation correct?
entries.answer: Code 1241 in the now outdated SAMA coding system was intended for use when a radiologist or physician performed fluoroscopy of the chest. This is no longer done and the code is therefore redundant. This may not be used, under any circumstances, for the viewing and/or reporting of a chest x-ray by a general practitioner or specialist.

entries.question: 1. Is there a code that Radiologists can charge for localised sedation – mostly done with certain MRI’s? 2. If a dislocated shoulder is located in Radiology, can the Radiologist charge a fee?
entries.answer: Both are outside the normal scope of practice of a specialized radiologist. I assume that you are talking about conscious sedation in your first question? Sedation given to patients to relax them in the MRI? We recommend that you use a pulse oximeter for these patients and you could charge for that. If you want to charge for conscious sedation, that is administration of the drugs and the monitoring of the patient, you would have to use a SAMA code at a GP rate but I wouldn’t recommend that unless the radiologist is in full time attendance and doing nothing else at the time. You could charge for relocating a shoulder but you would have to use a SAMA code at GP rates. Whether or not you would get paid is another issue?

entries.question: Is a radiologist allowed to share premises with a general practitioner? They would work independently and are not in association, partnership or other form of perverse incentive? I am sensitive to the fact that this would be interpreted as a perverse incentive by competitors in the market.
entries.answer: From the South African ethical rules, the relevant references are: "8A. Sharing of Rooms.—A practitioner shall not share his or her rooms with a person or entity not registered in terms of the Act. '“rooms” means a physical structure, with an exclusive entrance and walled all round for the privacy of patients, the preservation of their confidentiality and the safe keeping of records, where a practitioner conducts his or her practice;' There are no specific references in the Business Practice guidelines. The Act ,means the Health Professions Act, under which all doctors and dentists are registered. Pharmacist are registered under the Pharmacy Act. The definition of rooms is vague as it is not clear whether the exclusive entrance applies to the main consulting rooms entrance or the individual doctors entrance, but it is irrelevant as you can share rooms with doctors registered under the Health Professions Act. This is what happens at the Private Hospitals with attached consulting rooms and Medicross which houses GPs and radiologists. Convenience is not a perverse incentive.

entries.question: Can a foreign radiologist, not registered with HPCSA, issue a provisional radiological report in South Africa which is then followed by a final report by a HPCSA registered radiologist. I am quering this in relation to Private Practice. It would be similar to a registrar /consultant arrangement in State.
entries.answer: The issue is not the same as a registrar/consultant in the state as the state arrangement involves two medical practitioners both registered with the HPCSA and the registrar being in a recognized training program allowing him or her to do reporting on state patients under the supervision of a specialist radiologist. Your example refers to private practice. The arrangement you suggest is not allowed for several reasons: • The practitioner is not registered as a specialist radiologist in South Africa. • The practice is almost certainly a form of ‘ghosting’ as it is unlikely that the South African-based radiologist will give as much time to the report as he or she would have done otherwise. The risk here is that the South African radiologist will simply be using his qualification and registration to rubber stamp the report done by an unregistered practitioner. It is offered as a cheaper service as the foreign radiologist does most of the work. • Should be provisional report be used for clinical intervention it could result in all sorts of medico-legal problems involving both the referring practitioner as he or she would be associating with and an unregistered practitioner and potential problems for the South African radiologist as it would be questionable who would be providing the indemnity Cover.

entries.question: The Xray dept at La verna has installed a teleradiology system. What are the basic confidentiality requirements for such a system. We have monitors in O T ,wards, ICU and doctors rooms connected to our server Please advise
entries.answer: I understand from the email that you do not have a physical presence in the hospital, but the requirements are the same. The RSSA has produced advice on confidentiality which is attached. The RSSA position at present is that access to users of PACS and RIS should be protected by user name and password for each user. For users outside hospitals i.e. GPs or specialists in rooms not attached to a hospital, access should be restricted to the individual doctor’s patients. Within a hospital in is international best practice to allow access via individual user name and password to all the images related to patients using the hospital. This is necessary due to the cross referral between GPs, casualty and specialists. However all users should be made aware that from an ethical point of view a practitioner can only access information of patients he or she has a clinical responsibility for. In addition it is good protection to keep an audit trail of access and monitor it for unauthorised access. You should also have the ability to further restrict access if required. Having said that, I have received, in the last few days, an opinion from a lawyer briefed by a practice which states that under the National Health Act, the radiologist has an obligation to limit access of images only to the referring doctor, even in a hospital environment. We are having this opinion reviewed. If the opinion is correct it may be necessary for us to apply for exemption from the regulator. I will keep you and all members updated on this.

entries.question: THE ETHICAL RULES AND REGULATIONS
entries.answer: 553 Madiba Street Arcadia, Pretoria PO Box 205 Pretoria, 0001 Tel: +27 (12) 3389300 Fax: +27 (12) 3285120 Email: lizen@hpcsa.co.za Website: www.hpcsa.co.za Media Statement PRACTITIONERS WARNED ABOUT DUBIOUS MOBILE RADIOLOGY SERVICES 5 May 2014 For immediate release Radiology – 1 Pretoria – The Professional Board for Radiography and Clinical Technology of the Health Professions Council of South Africa (HPCSA) has warned practitioners about suspicious mobile radiology practices. It has come to the attention of the Board that some companies have purchased x-ray equipment for mobile radiology services. These companies are apparently struggling to obtain licenses from Radiation Control Directorate of the National Department of Health to use these machines as they are not adequately qualified and trained to operate such machines. They make lucrative offers to practitioners in order to use their HPCSA numbers, thereby securing licenses from Radiation Control. “With a mandate of protecting the public and guiding the professions, the Council is concerned that practitioners, and as a result the public, might be exposed to suspect operators,” Mable Kekana, Chairperson of the Professional Board for Radiography and Clinical Technology said. “Although the Board is not opposed to practitioners bringing healthcare services closer to communities through various mobile practices, the Board is concerned about the possible unethical and unprofessional relationship that might emanate between the healthcare practitioner and the company providing the service,” she explained. Practitioners are urged to be conversant with the type of private practice they want to engage in and to ensure that any type of practice adheres to the ethical rules and regulations* of the Council. Practitioners are furthermore advised to desist from giving or selling their HPCSA registration numbers or private practice numbers obtained from the Board of Healthcare Funders (BHF). Practitioners must be cognisant of the fact that they are liable for all professional acts performed or taking place in their presence or absence when their personal registration numbers are used. Radiology…2/ Radiology-2 The Board is also calling on practitioners who are aware of any unethical practices regarding mobile radiology practices to report these to the HPCSA for further investigation. *EXTRACT FROM THE ETHICAL RULES AND REGULATIONS: Professional appointments (1) A practitioner shall accept a professional appointment or employment from employers approved by the council only in accordance with a written contract of appointment or employment which is drawn up on a basis which is in the interest of the public and the profession. (2) A written contract of appointment or employment referred to in subrule (1) shall be made available to the council at its request. Partnership and juristic persons (1) A practitioner may practise in partnership or association with or employ only a practitioner who is registered under the Act and who is not prohibited under any of the annexures to these rules or any ethical rulings from entering into such partnership or association or being so employed: Provided that, in the case of employment, the practitioner so employed either provides a supportive health care service to complete or supplement the employing practitioner's healthcare or treatment intervention or is in the same professional category as the employing practitioner. (2) A practitioner shall practise in or as a juristic person who is exempted from registration in terms of section 54A of the Act only if such juristic person complies with the conditions of such exemption. (3) A practitioner shall practise in a partnership, association or as a juristic person only within the scope of the profession in respect of which he or she is registered under the Act. (4) A practitioner shall not practise in any other form of practice which has inherent requirements or conditions that violate or potentially may violate one or more of these rules or an annexure to these rules. Technological equipment (1) Health care practitioners shall only own and use technological equipment if it forms an integral part of their scope of the profession and practice and on condition that the health care practitioner concerned has received appropriate training in using and managing such equipment. (2) Health care practitioners shall not over-use equipment for procedures, tests and other applications that are not indicated, scientific or based on evidence. This constitutes overservicing and is prohibited. (3) Health care professionals shall not use technological equipment, health care products or devices for profiteering and must refrain from charging patients fees for the use of such products or devices that are not market related. Radiology…3/ Radiology-3 Financial interest in hospitals A practitioner may have a direct or indirect financial interest or shares in a hospital or any other health care institution: Provided that - (a) such interests or shares are purchased at market-related prices in arm's length transactions; (b) the purchase transaction or ownership of such interest or shares does not impose conditions or terms upon the practitioner that will detract from the good, ethical and safe practice of his or her profession; (c) the returns on investment or payment of dividends is not based on patient admissions or meeting particular targets in terms of servicing patients; (d) such practitioner does not over-service patients and to this end establishes appropriate peer review and clinical governance procedures for the treatment and servicing of his or her patients at such hospital or health care institution; (e) such practitioner does not participate in the advertising or promotion of the hospital or health care institution, or in any other activity that amounts to such advertising or promotion; (f) such practitioner does not engage in or advocate the preferential use of such hospital or health care institution; (g) the purchase agreement is approved by the council based on the criteria listed in paragraphs (a) to (f) above; and (h) such practitioner annually submit a report to the council indicating the number of patients referred by him or her or his or her associates or partners to such hospital or health care institution and the number of patients referred to other hospitals in which he or she or his or her associates or partners hold no shares. Rentals as perverse incentives Health care practitioners shall not: (1) Pay rentals in lease agreements between health care practitioners and health establishments that are not market related or are at preferential rates. (2) Enter into lease agreements with health establishments or services that wish to rent their consulting rooms at rates conditional on the health care practitioner achieving a certain turnover or targets such as admission of a specific number of patients at a private health care facility. (3) Rent consulting rooms from health establishments or services under financial arrangements that are not openly available to other similarly qualified health care practitioners. Radiology…4/ Radiology-4 Commission (1) Accepting commission Health care practitioners shall not accept commission or any financial gain or other valuable consideration from any person or body or service in return for the purchase, sale or supply of any goods, substances or materials used by the health care professional in his or her practice. (2) Paying commission Health care practitioners shall not pay commission or render any financial gain or other valuable consideration to any person for recommending patients. Charging or receiving fees (1) For referring patients Health care practitioners shall not charge a fee or receive any financial gain or other valuable consideration for referring patients to the other health professional or for participation in drug trials or other research trials of a similar nature. (2) For seeing representatives Health care practitioners shall not charge a fee or receive any financial gain or other valuable consideration for seeing medical representatives. (3) For services not personally rendered Health care practitioners shall not charge or receive fees for services not personally rendered by either a health care professional himself or herself or by an unregistered person in his or her employ, except for services rendered by another health care practitioner or person registered in terms of the Health Professions Act (Act No. 56 of 1974), that regulates the particular profession, with whom the health care practitioner is associated as a partner, shareholder or locum tenens. Sharing of fees Health care practitioners shall not share fees with any person or health care professional who has not taken a commensurate part in the service for which the fees are charged. Contracts (1) Health care practitioners shall not enter into a contract to work in a particular health establishment or service on the understanding that the health care professional generates a particular amount of revenue for such health establishment or service. [Note: A health establishment or service that equips a theatre, ward or other facility for a specific health care practitioner according to his or her specifications may enter into a contractual agreement with such health care professional on condition that such health establishment or service may not stipulate any turnover targets for the health care practitioner concerned]. Ends About the Health Professions Council of South Africa (HPCSA) The Health Professions Council of South Africa (HPCSA) is a statutory body and is committed to protecting the public and guiding the professions. The mission of the HPCSA is quality healthcare standards for all. The Council is mandated to regulate the health professions in the country in aspects pertaining to registration, education and training, professional conduct and ethical behaviour, ensuring continuing Professional Development (CPD), and fostering compliance with healthcare standards. In order to safeguard the public and indirectly the professions, registration in terms of the Act is a prerequisite for practising any of the health professions with which Council is concerned.

entries.question: Are we permitted to bill a patient for a consult if a referring specialist came to discuss a case and findings with a Radiologist for treatment planning? We also do a lot of special post processing for cochlear and BAHA implants work-up.
entries.answer: No to both, consolation and appropriate pst processing are included throughout.

entries.question: If a South African radiologist visits our congress and claims a certain amount (e.g. 20 credits) of CME points, which are UEMS/EACCME accredited, will they be accepted by the national jurisdiction in South Africa?
entries.answer: The last discussion I had, several years ago, was that HPCSA had stopped accrediting every international congress. I have submitted CME from international congresses in my last two audits without any objection.

entries.question: Could you please assist me with a query regarding patients that come to our department for chest x-rays for Home Affairs purposes. Would you be able to e-mail me some kind of proof (perhaps gazetted in the Government Gazette?) that says that patients have to pay cash for chest x-rays if they are for Home Affairs, and that medical aids are not allowed to be used for this purpose? We had an incident with a patient this afternoon that came for chest x-rays (he was already sick and referred by his GP to us for chest x-rays). The patient is on a medical aid. He wanted us to have his Home Affairs form stamped and signed (in case he went overseas within the next 6 months), i.e. kill two birds with one stone. We explained to him that all chest x-rays for Home Affairs purposes have to be paid for in cash, and not on medical aid. Dr Stoyanov was looking for some kind of proof so that we can provide this to the patient, but we were unable to find any. He therefore asked me to approach you in this regard and request that we be e-mailed some sort of document that explains this process to patients.
entries.answer: I am not aware of any legislation which covers this. I have discussed with Discovery and they make no distinction as then cannot tell from the account whether it is a Home Affairs chest or not. I imagine the other schemes are the same. In the case you mention, the decision on how to handle this is up to you. If you wanted to charge the patient extra, it would be a very reduced fee as the examination would already have been done and the diagnostic opinion given, all you would be doing is certifying a copy of the report on the Home Affairs form. In my own practice we would do this at no charge as a form of goodwill.

entries.question: I do Safety & Health files for companies that work on the mines from Brits to Rustenburg. In total about 38 mines. A few years back we also had the same issue of when is X-RAYS to much, as each and every single mine expect us to do a medical and x-rays. Is it possible to send me a letter stating what is the allowed frequency per person per year for x-rays. I have to do a new file every single year per company, and every company have about 6 mechanics and their apprentices. So we are doing more medicals and x-rays than what we are actually working. The moral is that the guys are refusing to do medicals as they say they are exposed to many x-rays. Your assistance regarding this issue is appreciated.
entries.answer: "Thank you for contacting us about this. This was discussed at a recent meeting between the Radiological Society (RSSA) and the Department of Health Radiation Protection Division. This involves both the Department of Health and whoever is responsible for the Mine Health Safety Act, presumably the Department of Labour. As far as I can see the Mine Health and Safety Act only requires a chest X-ray every three years, and I cannot see any need or reason for it to be repeated just because the worker moves between employers. The chest X-ray is portable and can be given to the worker or failing that a written report by a radiologist or an occupational health Physician should be sufficient. Repeating the the Chest X-ray at short intervals is unnecessary, potentially dangerous and probably financially motivated. The occupational health services should be able to contact the previous examiner. I do not have sufficient knowledge to give a definitive opinion on the legality, but from our point of view the Chest X-ray, if normal, and the patient has no symptoms, should not be repeated within a three year period. Fortunately, although unnecessary radiation is not recommended, the dose from a chest X-ray is very small so it is very unlikely that any harm will have been caused when these recommendations have been exceeded."

entries.question: I have received an enquiry (See red below) from a Radiographer in the public sector ,working at a Clinic (CHC) with X-ray Services , but no Radiologists to report on films. “Departmental practice. AP view of both limbs is taken, lateral view done for affected limb. The lateral view of the unaffected limb is done only if the growth plate of the affected limb appears abnormal. REFERRING DOCTORS IN COMMUNITY HEALTH CARE CENTRE They want us to do AP and LAT of both limbs immediately . Their reason is that they are not sure of a fracture or a growth plate. This means they cannot differentiate between the two.” • X-rays requests are referred by Doctors in CHC and there is a concern raised by the Radiographer that they are demanding AP and Lat views of both limbs of children because they may be unsure on how to differentiate between a fracture or a growth plate. • The Radiographer has a concern that vulnerable children are exposed to “unnecessary” radiation , because more views than required are requested. X-rays taken without accompanying X-ray reports is a common situation at Clinics with no Radiologists and no PACS/Teleradiology. I would not like Radiographers to put themselves at risk by limiting the x-ray requests on the basis of radiation protection concerns of children, when the request itself merits an optimal diagnostic result for referring doctors. I would like to request your input in this regard as the Radiological Society of South Africa .
entries.answer: The injured limb in a child, should be routinely examined with two views at right angles to each other (AP and lateral) The RSSA does not recommend or support comparative views of the contralateral limb when a radiologist is reporting the examination. When the case is not reported by a radiologist it may be necessary occasionally to take a single comparative view of the suspicious area of the uninjured limb. This should not be routine. Using modern, well-collimated equipment there is not significant radiation risk to the child.

entries.question: We need to know if there is any way you can assist us and other radiology practices in future for the use of A group practice name like for instance Rustenburg Radiology pty ltd - we can still list the partner names on stationary under the group name it will just help that we do not have to re register the practice name everytime we change directors And for memberships they can still have all the partners names on under a group practice name. It is such a huge problem to change a total registered name on creditors , medical aids, banks etc - it is a enourmas task while the name can stay the same and just partner names can be removed or added underneath the registered name on stationary Is it possible to discuss this with the minister of health or whom it may concern as it was allowed previously and now it is not allowed anymore
entries.answer: The RSSA took up this issue with the HPCSA, several years ago, and was unable to reach a satisfactory conclusion. There is no requirement for you to register your practice with HPCSA, the only registration is with the PCNS run by the Board of Healthcare Funders. We will be sending out an email to members on PCNS in the near future. The practices which are branding their practices with a name other than the names of their doctors, register the practice with PCNS as ‘Dr Smith and Partners' but trade under ‘Pampoenkraal Radiology’ as an example. To our knowledge there have been no complaints. Our colleagues in other specialities doe the same by trading under ‘Pathcare’, ’Surgicare’ etc. An incorporated practice has permanent succession so a name change is not required if the partners or radiologists change. I hope that this helps. Please contact me if you need further information.

entries.question: When is the full spine study with stress views and pelvis appropriate billing?
entries.answer: The full spine study with stress views and pelvis should NOT be billed in 100% of cases. Trauma, suspected osteoporotic fractures, suspected disc lesions, and back-ache where spondylolysis or spondylolisthesis is not suspected on the initial study hardly justifies full flexion and extension views. Apply clinical judgement – only bill for studies appropriate to the presenting symptoms and diagnosis.

entries.question: Can pelvis be routinely billed together with lumbar spine X-rays?
entries.answer: There is no indication for the routine addition of pelvis to a lumbar spine examination for acute or chronic backache. Pelvis may be added when there are clinical indications and specifically requested by the referring doctor, or at the radiologist's discretion with specific clinical justification. Verirad figures show the combination is used in 7–95% of cases; the national average is around 30%. Practices should assess their own profile against this benchmark. If the orthopaedic surgeon routinely requests pelvis, get a signed study protocol to provide to funders.

entries.question: When is contrast indicated for lumbar or cervical spine MRI?
entries.answer: MRI of the lumbar spine should NOT virtually always be done with contrast. Contrast is indicated for previous surgery, intra-spinal metastatic lesions, or other mass/inflammatory lesions. The indications for contrast in routine lumbar and cervical MRI for radicular symptoms or spinal stenosis are limited. The billing profile of any practice should reflect this – practices where contrast is used on nearly every spine MRI will attract scrutiny.

entries.question: Can the lateral spine view produced during a bone densitometry (BMD) scan be billed separately?
entries.answer: No. Some models of bone densitometry screening equipment produce a lateral spine view at the time of the BMD study. This is considered to be part of the BMD study and separate billing for this image as a spine view is NOT acceptable.

entries.question: What are the billing rules for skeletal survey? Can I bill individual body part codes?
entries.answer: Codes 00110 + 00115 describe a standard skeletal survey. Practices must NOT bill all the individual codes for the different body parts included in the study (e.g. Skull, cervical spine, thoracic spine, lumbar spine, pelvis etc.) as separate billing lines. Such billing is not acceptable and is not supported by RSSA.

entries.question: Which code should I use for CT of the paranasal sinuses?
entries.answer:
CT paranasal sinuses billing (updated 2022/2023):
• 13300 – CT paranasal sinuses, single plane, limited study: use for routine screening when referred by ANY practitioner OTHER than an ENT surgeon. Replaces conventional sinus X-rays. Any GP or specialist (non-ENT) can request this.
• 13320 – CT paranasal sinuses, any plane, complete: use when patient is referred by an ENT surgeon. Radiologist provides additional planes at no extra cost.
• 13330 – Reserved for exceptionally complex pre-surgery planning cases only. Most paranasal sinuses should be billed as 13300 or 13320.
• 13340 – CT paranasal sinuses complete with contrast: when referred by a specialist with indication for contrast and MRI is unavailable or contraindicated.
Note: The fact that MDCT allows multiplane reconstruction from one dataset does NOT justify billing for multiple planes when there is no change in clinical indication.

entries.question: When should diffusion MRI of the brain be billed?
entries.answer:
Diffusion MRI brain should NOT be billed routinely in a high percentage of brain studies. Limit billing for brain plus diffusion to cases where:
• Acute ischaemia is a real clinical possibility
• MS
• Abscesses and other inflammatory conditions
• Epidermoid tumours
• Some trauma cases
The widespread routine use of diffusion has significantly increased MRI spend and brought MRI fees under intense scrutiny by funders.

entries.question: When is CT brain pre and post contrast (with contrast) appropriate?
entries.answer: The indications for CT brain pre and post contrast have shrunk significantly with modern CT and the widespread use of MRI. Be circumspect when justifying contrast for a CT brain study, especially in routine cases like trauma, acute-phase ischaemic incident, or headache – particularly when an MRI is likely to follow.

entries.question: When is a full knee study with patella view justified versus a 2-view study?
entries.answer: A 2-view knee study is appropriate for many cases and should be performed – it is difficult to justify the full knee study with patella in all cases, especially for younger patients with minor trauma or where the request is 'knee pain? OA'. If standing views are required, the AP view can be done standing as part of the knee study – a separate fee for an additional standing AP view is NOT supported. Bilateral knees standing on one film do not justify an additional comparative view fee.

entries.question: Can the calcaneus be billed separately when foot views are done?
entries.answer: The calcaneus view code is for cases where ONLY the calcaneus is imaged. The foot view already includes the calcaneus. Additional billing for calcaneus with foot views CANNOT be supported except in exceptional circumstances that must be motivated. Remember the swings-and-roundabouts principle: a lesser fee does not apply when only 1-2 views of the foot are done; conversely, in exceptional cases needing more than 3 views, the standard fee remains appropriate overall.

entries.question: How should paediatric arm and leg imaging be billed when multiple regions fit on one or two films?
entries.answer: When two films cover a limb from hip to foot or shoulder to hand, practices must NOT bill separately for every joint and bone included in those films. Although no single code currently exists for these studies, billing ethics should lead to charging for ONE of the joints or set of bones rather than 5-6 separate billing lines. Future coding updates will include single codes for these studies. Note: Comparative views of the contralateral limb are NOT indicated when a radiologist is reporting the examination.

entries.question: When should code 40210 (combined ultrasound abdomen and pelvis) be used?
entries.answer: Code 40210 (combined ultrasound abdomen and pelvis) should NOT be billed as a routine in all abdominal scans. Evaluation of the pelvis can generally be justified for female patients. There is little justification for billing this code for a male presenting with suspected gall stones. If the bladder is empty and very limited pelvic evaluation is possible – especially when the suspected pathology is in the upper abdomen – the additional billing for the pelvis is questionable.

entries.question: What is the correct code for renal stones or suspected renal colic – 42300 or 40337?
entries.answer: Code 42300 (CT of the renal tract for a stone / CT KUB) is the standard code for suspected renal tract obstruction by a stone. The vast majority of cases can be adequately evaluated WITHOUT intravenous contrast. Code 40337 (CT abdomen and pelvis pre and post contrast) should NOT be used routinely for suspected renal stones – this is not supported as the norm. Exception: if no stone is found on the unenhanced scan and there is painless haematuria, a contrasted study may be appropriate (CT IVU / code 40313). If there is confusion between a distal ureteric stone and a phlebolith, a small volume of IV contrast (10-20 ml) with repeat pelvic imaging 10-15 minutes later is one solution.

entries.question: When should code 20220 versus 20230 be used for carotid Doppler studies?
entries.answer: Code 20220 is the standard study for routine carotid Doppler – includes carotid and vertebrals bilaterally. This is the code used by the vast majority of practices for routine carotid studies. Code 20230 was originally intended for very limited use: evaluation of transcranial vascular malformations and fistulas as part of surgical or interventional work-up. It should NOT be used for routine Doppler studies. Note: there is no separate code for temporal artery Doppler – the extracranial vascular tree including temporal arteries is included in code 20220.

entries.question: Can fluoroscopy code 00140 be billed as part of a plain X-ray study where positioning is difficult?
entries.answer: No. Fluoroscopy code 00140 CANNOT be billed as part of a B&W X-ray study where positioning is difficult. This has been discussed at RSSA Exco level and is NOT supported at all by RSSA.

entries.question: Is CAD mammography billing supported by RSSA?
entries.answer: No. RSSA has NOT supported the CAD Mammo code for several years and applied for its deletion as early as 2006. Some funders, notably Discovery Health, have stopped paying for this code. RSSA is not in a position to challenge those decisions. There is also no additional fee chargeable for tomosynthesis at present.

entries.question: What are the rules for billing emergency call out codes 01010 and 01020?
entries.answer:
Code 01010 – Emergency callout:
• Only used when a radiologist is called out TO THE ROOMS to report after normal working hours.
• May NOT be used for routine reporting during extended working hours.
• Charged ONCE per callout for the FIRST modality reported (plain film, CT, US, MR etc.), regardless of the number of examinations in that modality.

Code 01020 – Subsequent cases:
• Used when reporting subsequent cases after having been called out for an initial after-hours procedure. Also used for HOME TELE-RADIOLOGY reporting of an emergency procedure.
• May NOT be used for routine reporting during normal or extended working hours.
• May be charged once per MODALITY for the same patient during the same callout.
• May be charged once per modality for subsequent PATIENTS during the same callout.
• May be charged once per modality for telemedicine reporting.

Definitions:
• Normal working hours: 08:30–17:00 Mon–Fri, 08:30–12:00 Saturday
• Extended hours: routine extensions of these hours in the rooms
• After hours: all hours outside these times and public holidays

Example – In one callout to rooms:
Patient A, X-rays: 01010 × 1
Patient A, CT: 01020 × 1
